prompt: Create a clean flat-vector infographic poster in a portrait orientation, divided into two equal-width vertical columns separated only by a change in background colors rather than by visible lines. The top half of the left column is a solid dark teal rectangle containing a large centered illustration at the top of a person sitting behind a simple curved line representing a bed edge, arms stretched straight up as if waking, drawn in a flat cartoon style with thin outlines, muted peach skin, long dark brown hair, and loose light blue sleepwear. Beneath this figure, center the four-line all-caps main title “6 REASONS TO GET UP EARLY” in bold white sans-serif letters, with “EARLY” on the last line set noticeably larger than the other words. The middle of the left column is a white rectangle for the “Less time in traffic” section: on its right two-thirds place a left-aligned heading “Less time in traffic” in dark teal bold sans-serif, with a smaller paragraph of dark gray regular-weight sans-serif text underneath explaining the benefit; on the lower-left of this panel show a waist-up person in flat style, with dark hair, a light blue long-sleeved top, one hand holding a pale yellow-green folder and the other forming an OK gesture. The bottom of the left column is a light aqua-blue rectangle for “Helps you concentrate”, with a left-aligned dark teal bold sans-serif heading of that phrase and several lines of dark gray regular-weight sans-serif body text below; on the right side of this panel draw a waist-up character with dark hair and a light T-shirt giving two thumbs up, rendered in the same flat, solid-fill, minimal-facial-detail style. The right column is stacked with three main horizontal bands and one overlapping sub-panel: the upper-right band is a light beige rectangle containing the “More time for yourself” section, whose left side holds a dark teal bold sans-serif heading “More time for yourself” above a dark gray regular-weight paragraph about getting personal time; on the right, add an athletic character wearing a dark purple sports bra and shorts with peach skin and long dark hair, arms raised and flexed, positioned so that her torso overlaps downward into the dark brown panel below. Immediately under the beige panel, spanning the width of the right column but shorter in height, place a solid dark brown rectangle for the “More time to exercise” section; inside it, left-align a bold sans-serif light beige heading “More time to exercise” with a smaller paragraph of matching light beige body text beneath, while allowing the upper part of the same sporty character from above to intrude into the top edge of this brown area. Below this, create a light warm gray rectangle for the “More time to get things done” section: near the top-left write the heading “More time to get things done” in bold sans-serif dark brown, followed by a left-aligned paragraph of dark gray regular-weight text; centered in the panel draw a person with dark hair wearing a muted greenish-gray shirt, looking at and holding a smartphone with both hands, and around this character arrange three small white circles connected by thin curved light gray lines, each circle containing a flat icon—a receipt labeled “PAID!” in green, a steaming coffee cup, and a brown grocery bag with its contents visible. The bottom-right band is a very light pink-beige rectangle for the “Healthier skin” section, featuring a dark teal bold sans-serif heading “Healthier skin” and a paragraph of dark gray regular-weight sans-serif text beneath it; on the right side, include a character in a short-sleeved red top, dark hair, arms crossed over the chest, with several small solid pink heart icons floating around the head and shoulders. Ensure there are exactly six titled reason sections plus the main title area, that all characters share a consistent flat vector appearance with solid color fills, thin outlines, and minimal facial detail, that body text is always dark gray and smaller than headings, and that all background panels and icons use uniform solid colors with no gradients, textures, or extra decorative borders.

prompt: Create a vertical, poster-style family tree infographic on a solid light yellowish-beige background, featuring a large, semi-transparent desaturated light sage green tree graphic centered in the composition, with a rounded leaf canopy in the upper middle, a straight trunk along the central vertical axis, and branching pale green roots spreading out above dark forest-green hill silhouettes that form an irregular wavy band along the bottom edge. Near the top, place a wide wooden plank sign shaped as a slightly irregular rounded rectangle spanning about two-thirds of the width, rendered in warm medium brown with darker brown shading along the lower and side edges and lighter tan highlights along the top to suggest a beveled 3D plank; inside this plank, write “FAMILY TREE” in large all-caps bold sans-serif letters, light cream in fill with a darker brown outline and subtle inner shading so the text appears slightly raised. Attach two vertical metallic light gray chains made from small oval links, one on each side of the plank, running straight upward until they meet the top border of the image. Centered below the plank, add the subheading “My Ancestral Family Tree” in a single line of medium-dark brown sentence-case sans-serif text with medium weight and moderate spacing. Overlaying the tree graphic, arrange nine circular portrait frames in three rows: the top row has two circles, the middle row four circles, and the bottom row three circles. Make every circle exactly the same size with a thick medium-green outer border and a thin white inner ring, with the interior filled by a portrait photo; ensure all circles are perfectly round and aligned horizontally within each row. In the top row, place two circles symmetrically left and right, and beneath them center dark brown captions in a medium-weight serif font, each caption enclosed in curly braces with spaces inside so they read exactly “{ Grandfather }” under the left circle and “{ Grandmother }” under the right circle. In the second row, directly beneath the canopy center, place four equally spaced circles and caption them from left to right in the same serif style and dark brown color as “{ Auntie }”, “{ Mom }”, “{ Dad }”, and “{ Uncle }”, with each label centered below its circle and bracketed with curly braces and inner spaces. In the bottom row, place three circles horizontally in the lower-middle area so that the central circle aligns with the tree trunk; caption them from left to right as “{ Little Brother }”, “{ Big Sister }”, and “{ My Self }” using the same serif type, dark brown color, and spaced curly braces, each centered directly beneath its circle. Draw family relationships using thin, solid medium-green connector lines and arrows that match the circle borders: between the two top grandparent circles, draw a straight horizontal double-headed arrow whose arrowheads touch the outer edges of each circle; from the midpoint of this arrow drop a short vertical arrow pointing downward to a straight horizontal connector line positioned just above the four parent circles; from this horizontal line, draw four vertical arrows downward, each ending at the top of its respective parent circle. From the bottom of the circle labeled “{ Dad }”, draw a vertical arrow straight down to the top of the central bottom circle labeled “{ Big Sister }”. Finally, from the midpoint of each side of the central bottom circle, extend short horizontal green line segments without arrowheads to the left and right to meet the adjacent bottom circles labeled “{ Little Brother }” and “{ My Self }”, respectively, so the connectors and aligned circles visually merge with the pale sage tree trunk and roots to create a coherent family tree illustration.

prompt: Create a clean vertical team timeline infographic on a tall canvas with a very light gray background contained inside a large rounded rectangle with heavily rounded corners and a thin light gray border. Place the title word “Timeline” in the upper left, in large bold black modern sans-serif font similar to Poppins, left-aligned. Down the exact vertical center of the canvas, draw a single thin solid black line from near the top margin to near the bottom; this is the main timeline spine. Along this line place six equally spaced solid-colored circular markers, each bisected by the line and containing a centered bold white numeral from 1 to 6 arranged in ascending order from top to bottom. Give marker 1 and its matching card color a medium violet, marker 2 the same medium violet, marker 3 a bright red, marker 4 a saturated orange, marker 5 a rich medium-dark green, and marker 6 a warm golden yellow. From each numbered circle, draw a thin solid horizontal connector line in exactly the same color as the circle toward its corresponding information card, alternating sides so that markers 1, 3, and 5 connect to cards on the right and markers 2, 4, and 6 connect to cards on the left. On each side, create large horizontal rounded rectangles with very large corner radii, forming pill-shaped cards; each card is filled with its respective solid color with no gradients and no shadows. Position the first violet card on the upper right, the second violet card slightly lower on the upper left, the bright red card at mid height on the right, the orange card at mid height on the left, the green card on the lower right, and the yellow card on the lower left near the bottom, aligning each card with its connector line. On every card, center a circular portrait photo at the top so that about half of the portrait circle extends above the top edge of the card and half overlaps the rectangle; surround each portrait with a thin white circular border that clearly separates it from the card color. Inside each card, place a single line of all-caps name text (for example “CIA RODRIGUEZ”, “HELENE PAQUET”, “ROSA MARIA AGUADO”, “KIMBERLY NGUYEN”, “SHAWN GARCIA”, “NOAH SCHUMACHER”) in bold white sans-serif font aligned left and positioned just below the overlapping portrait, followed by several lines of smaller regular-weight white lorem ipsum paragraph text, also left-aligned, to describe the team member. Maintain consistent typography and spacing across all six cards so that the names share the same font size and the body text shares the same smaller size. Beneath the timeline on the lower right of the canvas, add a two-line text block reading “Meet the team” on the first line and “behind the brand” on the second line in black modern sans-serif font; make the first line bold and the second line regular, both left-aligned. Ensure the overall layout is symmetric around the central black timeline line, with alternating colored cards and their matching numbered circles and connector lines creating a clear, structured team timeline design.

prompt: Create a wide, landscape-oriented vector infographic with a flat, modern style and no gradients or transparency, explaining the utilization of maggots for organic waste decomposition and turning food waste into marketable value. Divide the background into three full-width horizontal bands: a medium grass-green top band, a pure white central band, and a light warm beige bottom band. In the green band, center a long pill-shaped horizontal banner filled with solid red-orange; inside it place white sentence-case sans-serif text reading “Utilization of Maggots for Organic Waste Decomposition.” Directly beneath this pill, still inside the green band, center the main title “TURNING FOOD WASTE INTO MARKETABLE VALUE” on three lines in very large, bold, all-caps white sans-serif letters. Scatter flat food-waste illustrations around the header within the green band: on the upper left, show a cream-colored plate seen from above with a warm peach-colored hand holding a brown fork over leftover scraps; on the right and near the top edge, add small icons like an orange bell-pepper top, a pale yellow cheese-like wedge with greenish spots, a beige bread crust, and a simple light-colored leaf skeleton, all drawn as solid-color shapes with smooth curves and no outlines. In the central white band, depict a six-step process arranged in two rows of three steps. For each step, place a circular badge and an associated content card. Draw six opaque circles containing centered white sans-serif text “Step 1” through “Step 6”; fill the odd-numbered circles (1, 3, and 5) with a solid medium green and the even-numbered circles (2, 4, and 6) with a warm orange. Arrange steps 1, 2, and 3 on the upper row from left to right and steps 4, 5, and 6 on the lower row from left to right. Connect the circles with thin light-gray dashed lines that form a continuous flow from step 1 through step 6; let the dashed line run horizontally across steps 1–3, then curve downward and left into the second row before running horizontally across steps 4–6. At the point where the dashed line curves between step 3 and step 4, place a medium-sized solid orange circle containing a bold white downward-pointing arrow, indicating the direction of flow. Beside each step circle, include a light beige rounded-rectangle card containing a small flat illustration and text. For step 1, label the heading above its paragraph in dark gray bold uppercase sans-serif as “VERMICOMPOST BIN PREPARATION,” followed by regular dark gray body text describing how to acquire or create a suitable plastic or wooden vermicompost bin with good ventilation and a lid; inside its beige card, show a simple wooden crate-style bin. For step 2, use the heading “ORGANIC WASTE COLLECTION” in the same bold uppercase dark gray style, with regular sans-serif body text explaining the dedicated container for organic waste such as food scraps, fruit peels, unused vegetables, and other organic materials; depict on its beige card a small bucket or container filled with colorful food scraps. For step 3, title it “MAGGOT PREPARATION” in bold uppercase dark gray, with a paragraph of regular dark gray text explaining obtaining fly larvae (maggots) from decomposing organic waste or specialized sources and ensuring they can efficiently decompose organic matter; on its beige card, draw several simple cream-colored larvae shapes with small brown heads. For step 4, use the heading “HABITAT PROVISION” in bold uppercase dark gray with a regular-weight paragraph explaining filling the vermicompost bin with collected food scraps and adding the fly larvae; illustrate its beige card with a person standing beside or leaning over a rectangular bin filled with material. For step 5, title it “MAINTENANCE” in bold uppercase dark gray and under the heading include three separate bullet lines of regular dark gray sans-serif text, each starting with a small solid black checkmark icon: one line about keeping the vermicompost adequately moist and occasionally spraying water if too dry, one about adding fresh organic material regularly in small amounts for efficient processing, and one about sealing the container tightly to prevent adult flies from escaping and to provide the larvae’s preferred dark conditions; in its beige card, show a person tending the bin. For step 6, set the heading “MONITORING AND UTILIZATION” in bold uppercase dark gray and follow it with a single paragraph in regular dark gray sans-serif describing monitoring the development of the fly larvae and compost and noting that the larvae decompose organic matter into compost within weeks to months; illustrate its beige card with a compost-filled bin and simple plants or greenery nearby. In all steps, ensure the headings are uppercase bold sans-serif, the body text is regular sans-serif in dark gray or black, and the illustrations are small, flat, and fully opaque. In the bottom light beige band, create a “Benefits of Maggots” section: on the left side, place a left-aligned section title reading “Benefits of Maggots” in bold black sans-serif text. To the right of this heading, lay out three equal-width vertical benefit columns. At the top of each column, include a small flat illustration, and below it place a bold uppercase sans-serif benefit title and a regular dark gray paragraph explaining the benefit. Title the first column “FISH FEED” and show a person at a rectangular fish tank or pond feeding fish, with accompanying text explaining that maggots are an excellent protein source and are used as feed for cultured fish such as carp, catfish, and tilapia. Title the second column “LIVESTOCK FEED” and illustrate a person feeding farm animals like pigs or poultry, with text stating that maggots are also used as livestock feed for poultry, ducks, and pigs. Title the third column “ORGANIC FERTILIZER” and show a compost heap or pile with organic matter and plants, with text explaining that maggots decompose organic waste, including food scraps, into nutrient-rich compost that enhances soil fertility in agriculture and gardening. Ensure all fonts across the infographic are clean sans-serif, using size and weight (bold for headings, regular for body) for hierarchy, keep all body text in dark gray or black, and maintain a consistent flat illustration style with solid colors, no gradients, and no transparency.

prompt: Create a flat, vector-style educational infographic on magnetic forces with a solid medium teal rectangular background and two opaque white vertical panels centered on it, the left panel occupying about three-fifths of the width and the right panel about two-fifths. At the top of the left panel, place the centered title “MAGNETIC FORCES” in very large, bold, uppercase black sans-serif letters. Directly below this title, add a wide horizontal definition box with rounded corners filled in light salmon red; inside this box, write a single English sentence defining magnetic force in black regular-weight sans-serif text, with the words “push” and “pull” in bold, and leave the box borderless. Beneath the box, fill the upper-middle of the left panel with several lines of left-aligned black body text in regular sans-serif, explaining magnets and atoms and occasionally bolding key terms such as “atoms”; do not use underlines or colored text for this paragraph block. In the lower-left area of the left panel, draw an atom diagram: a single thin light gray circular orbit containing exactly six small solid light teal electron circles evenly spaced around the ring, each electron marked with a tiny black minus sign; at the center, create a nucleus as an overlapping cluster of light gray and pale teal circles. To the right of the nucleus, place a black text label that reads “nucleus containing protons and neutrons,” using regular sans-serif with the words “protons and neutrons” in bold, and connect this label to the nucleus with a thin solid black horizontal leader line ending in a filled triangular arrowhead. On the right panel, add at the very top a full-width horizontal banner strip filled with very light cyan and no border; center the heading “MAGNETIC PROPERTIES” in bold uppercase black sans-serif within this strip. Below this sentence, center a bar magnet diagram: a horizontal rounded rectangle split exactly in half by a vertical line, with the left half filled light salmon red and the right half filled light sky blue; inside each half, draw a smaller white rectangle containing a bold black uppercase letter, “N” on the red side and “S” on the blue side. Above the left end of the magnet, write “north pole” in small black sans-serif and connect it to the left end using a thin solid black horizontal arrow pointing right; similarly, above the right end write “south pole” and connect it with a thin solid black arrow pointing left toward the blue half. Farther down the right panel, left-align the heading “PUSH FORCE” in bold uppercase black sans-serif, followed by a short regular-weight paragraph in black explaining that identical poles repel. To the right of this text, draw exactly two vertically stacked diagrams, each composed of a pair of parallel horizontal bar magnets colored and labeled identically to the main magnet (red N on the left, blue S on the right) such that like poles face each other in the central gap; surround each pair with multiple thin light gray curved field lines arching around the magnets and bending away from the central gap to visually indicate repulsion. Beneath this, left-align the heading “PULL FORCE” in the same bold uppercase black sans-serif style, with a regular-weight paragraph describing how opposite poles attract; to the right of this text, draw exactly two more vertically stacked diagrams, again each containing a pair of red–blue bar magnets with N and S labels, but this time arranged so that opposite poles face each other in the central gap, and surround them with thin light gray curved magnetic field lines that bridge across the gap to suggest attraction. prompt: Create a clean, modern multi-panel dashboard on a white background with thin light-gray panel borders and consistent dark-gray sans-serif typography; place panel labels “(A)” “(B)” “(C)” “(D)” “(E)” “(F)” “(G)” in the top-left corner of each respective panel. Do not include any data table in the image. Layout contract seven_header_body_footer_with_insets: (B) is a full-width header band at the very top; directly below it, (C), (A), and (D) form one middle row where (A) is the large central body panel, (C) is a left rail, and (D) is a right rail; (E) is a full-width footer band at the very bottom; (F) and (G) are inset panels inside (A) at opposite corners. Spatial constraints (must be followed): (1) (B) is above (C), (A), and (D) and aligned to their outer left and outer right edges; (2) (E) is below (C), (A), and (D) and aligned to the same outer left and outer right edges as (B); (3) (C) is immediately left of (A) and vertically aligned with (A); (4) (D) is immediately right of (A) and vertically aligned with (A); (5) (C) and (D) have equal height and their top edges align with the top edge of (A); (6) (C) and (D) are narrower sidebars than (A), creating an asymmetric mosaic with a dominant center; (7) (F) is inset inside (A) near the top-left corner without leaving (A)’s boundary, overlapping the treegraph’s whitespace but not covering the root node; (8) (G) is inset inside (A) near the bottom-right corner, opposite (F), and it must not overlap the footer band (E); (9) the title inside (A) is centered at the top of (A) and must sit below the top border of (A) and below the inset (F) so text never collides; (10) all panel labels remain visible and not obstructed by chart elements. Panel (A) primary chart content (must preserve the original treegraph content and values): draw a hierarchical treegraph with a box layout styled like a horizontal mind map; include the bold title centered at the top of (A): “World regions treegraph with population index”; place a single bright blue rounded rectangle node labeled “The World (Population index: 1000)” slightly left of center within (A); from this root node draw five smooth light-gray curved connector lines to five first-level region nodes arranged vertically in the middle of (A): orange “America (200)”, slate-blue “Europe (180)”, vivid green “Africa (260)”, deep violet “Asia (320)”, and light magenta “Oceanic (40)”; from each region node draw additional light-gray curved connectors to its subregion nodes in a vertical column to the right of that region, colored in a similar hue to their parent: America subregions “Northern America (70)”, “Central America (40)”, “Caribbean (30)”, “South America (60)”; Europe subregions “Western Europe (60)”, “Southern Europe (40)”, “Northern Europe (30)”, “Eastern Europe (50)”; Africa subregions “North Africa (70)”, “West Africa (60)”, “Central Africa (40)”, “East Africa (50)”, “South Africa (40)”; Asia subregions “Western Asia (60)”, “Central Asia (40)”, “Eastern Asia (90)”, “South-Eastern Asia (70)”, “Southern Asia (60)”; Oceanic subregions “Australia and New Zealand (20)”, “Melanesia (8)”, “Micronesia (6)”, “Polynesia (6)”; use white text inside colored boxes, no axes and no legend, and keep connectors light-gray and unobtrusive; only add a small number of extra numeric callouts in (A) (e.g., tiny labels near two region nodes and one subregion node) to aid readability without clutter. Panel (B) header band: include a concise dashboard title left-aligned within (B): “Population index dashboard” and a small right-aligned subtitle “World=1000”; keep (B) visually minimal with no chart, just text. Panel (C) left rail (derived summary): show a vertical stack of compact KPI cards (rounded rectangles) listing totals with large numbers and small labels, using the same region colors as (A): “World 1000”, “Asia 320”, “Africa 260”, “America 200”, “Europe 180”, “Oceanic 40”; align the KPI cards in one column and include only these numbers. Panel (D) right rail (derived comparison): draw a horizontal bar chart ranking the five regions by population index (Asia 320, Africa 260, America 200, Europe 180, Oceanic 40), with bars colored to match (A), category names on the left, and end-of-bar numeric labels; keep axes minimal (light-gray baseline only) and include at most one small tick annotation for scale. Panel (F) inset inside (A) (derived share view): a small donut chart of region shares of World=1000 using the same region colors, with 2–3 readable percentage labels (e.g., Asia 32%, Africa 26%, and one other) and a center label “World 1000”; no legend—direct labels only. Panel (G) inset inside (A) (derived diagnostic slice): a small stacked bar (single bar) breaking down Asia’s subregions exactly (Western 60, Central 40, Eastern 90, South-Eastern 70, Southern 60) with segment labels on only a few segments (e.g., 90, 70, 60) to avoid clutter, and a top label “Asia total 320”. Panel (E) footer band: keep it sparse; place “Source: Example data” in small light-gray text at the bottom-right of (E) and a short note bottom-left “All values are population index” in light-gray; no additional charts in (E).

Do not include any data table in the image. Enforce these spatial constraints: the top tier (A) is to the left of (B) and both are above the entire middle tier; the middle tier is a single horizontal row with (C) left of (D) left of (E); the bottom tier has (F) to the left of (G) and both are below the entire middle tier; center the top tier as a group above the middle tier, and center the middle tier as a group above the bottom tier so the tiers form a visually descending cascade; align the top edges of (A) and (B) to each other; align the top edges of (C),(D),(E) to each other; align the bottom edges of (F) and (G) to each other; align the vertical centerline of (D) with the overall dashboard centerline; align the left edges of (A),(C),(F) to form a clean left column; align the right edges of (B),(E),(G) to form a clean right column. Add a non-trivial structural pattern inside panel (D): include a small inset donut chart placed inside (D) at the bottom-right corner (contained within (D) without touching its border) showing continent shares of 7800 using the same continent colors, and label only a few key values on the donut (e.g., Asia 4600, Africa 1300, Europe 750) to avoid clutter. Panel (A) (top-left) is a KPI/summary strip: show the headline 'World Population by Region (millions)' and three large KPIs stacked vertically with minimal text: Total 7800, Largest continent Asia 4600, Smallest continent Oceanic 150; keep numbers prominent and include a small note 'units: millions' beneath. Panel (B) (top-right) is a horizontal bar chart comparing continent totals with five bars in the same continent colors ordered from largest to smallest (Asia 4600, Africa 1300, America 1000, Europe 750, Oceanic 150); place the bar-end numeric labels for each bar but no extra annotations, and keep axes minimal (light ticks only, no grid). Panel (C) (middle-left) is a treemap-style mosaic of continent totals: five rectangles packed tightly, each colored by continent with the continent name centered; include only two numeric labels inside the two largest rectangles (Asia 4600, Africa 1300) to keep readability. Panel (E) (middle-right) is a ranked lollipop (or vertical bar) chart of Asia subregions only using the Asia deep blue: Southern Asia 1900, Eastern Asia 1600, South-Eastern Asia 670, Western Asia 330, Central Asia 100; label each marker/bar with its value and keep the title 'Asia subregions (millions)'. Panel (F) (bottom-left) is a compact stacked bar showing Africa subregions composition summing to Africa 1300 using varying tints of the Africa green; show segment labels for only three segments (Eastern Africa 430, Western Africa 360, Northern Africa 250) and keep the remaining segment names as a legend within the panel. Panel (G) (bottom-right) is a small comparison chart: a scatterplot with one point per continent where x = number of subregions (Asia 5, Africa 5, America 4, Europe 4, Oceanic 4) and y = continent total (4600,1300,1000,750,150), with points colored by continent; label only Asia (5,4600) and Oceanic (4,150) directly on the plot, and keep other labels in a small legend inside (G). prompt: Create a wide, high-resolution infographic dashboard on a white background using a clean sans-serif font, with exactly six labeled panels: (A),(B),(C),(D),(E),(F); place each panel label in the top-left corner of its panel. Do not include any data table in the image. Layout contract six_inset_outer_with_bottom_triplet: Panel (A) is one large outer panel occupying the top portion; panel (B) is an inset inside (A) at (A)’s upper-right; panel (C) is an inset inside (A) at (A)’s lower-left; panels (D),(E),(F) form a bottom strip beneath (A), ordered left-to-right as (D) then (E) then (F). Spatial constraints (must all be satisfied): (1) (A) is above the entire bottom strip (D)(E)(F); (2) (D),(E),(F) are horizontally aligned in one row and share the same height; (3) (D) is left of (E), and (E) is left of (F); (4) the left edges of (D) and (A) are aligned; (5) the right edges of (F) and (A) are aligned; (6) (B) is fully contained within (A) and anchored to the upper-right corner of (A); (7) (C) is fully contained within (A) and anchored to the lower-left corner of (A); (8) (B) does not overlap the main title/subtitle area of (A) and sits below the title band within (A); (9) (C) does not cover (A)’s y-axis labels and does not cover the x-axis month labels; (10) keep visible padding so the bars in (A) remain fully readable around both insets; (11) the bottom strip is separated from (A) by a thin horizontal divider line spanning under (A); (12) any callout boxes in (A) may slightly overlap bars but must not overlap the inset panels (B) or (C). Panel (A) (primary chart; keep the original content as the main focus): a vertical diverging bar chart titled “Should I buy the new €29 ticket?” with subtitle “How much more or less I spent on local public transport in the past two years compared with a €29 monthly ticket.” X-axis shows months from “Sep 2020” through “Sep 2022”; y-axis labeled “Difference vs €29 monthly ticket (EUR)” ranging from -40 to +50 with light grey horizontal gridlines every 20 and a bold horizontal zero line. One vertical bar per month centered on its tick, positives extend upward, negatives downward, with a clearly readable numeric label at the tip of each bar including sign, using these exact values: Sep 2020 +47.9, Oct 2020 +38.2, Nov 2020 +34.4, Dec 2020 +37.8, Jan 2021 +38, Feb 2021 -23, Mar 2021 -29, Apr 2021 -7.8, May 2021 -21.4, Jun 2021 -1.7, Jul 2021 +0.4, Aug 2021 +41, Sep 2021 -3.4, Oct 2021 -5, Nov 2021 +1.8, Dec 2021 +3, Jan 2022 -15.8, Feb 2022 -19.6, Mar 2022 +24, Apr 2022 -13.6, May 2022 -15.4, Jun 2022 -15.8, Jul 2022 -20, Aug 2022 -20, Sep 2022 +4. Color code bars by ticket regime: medium grey for Sep 2020–Jan 2021 “Yearly subscription”; dark teal for “Pay-as-you-go cheaper than €29” months (Feb 2021–Jun 2021, Sep 2021, Oct 2021, Jan 2022, Feb 2022, Apr 2022, May 2022, Jun 2022); vivid red for “Pay-as-you-go more expensive than €29” months excluding subscription (Jul 2021, Aug 2021, Nov 2021, Dec 2021, Mar 2022, Sep 2022); light grey for “Special €9 ticket” months (Jul 2022, Aug 2022). prompt: Create a high-resolution, white-background scientific dashboard with thin black axes, no gridlines, and four labeled panels arranged exactly as follows: (A) spans the full top tier; directly below it is a single horizontal strip of three equal panels (B), (C), (D) from left to right. Observations: show solid black filled circles at x where Observation=Yes; highlight the newest observation in each relevant time as a red-outlined circle filled white and annotate it. Use smooth interpolation between the discrete points x=0,1,2,3,4 for each curve. Use these exact values: for t=2, μ=[0.0,0.3,-0.2,0.4,0.5], σ=[0.1,0.4,0.1,0.5,0.6], u=[0.05,0.20,0.05,0.35,0.30], observations at x=0 and x=2, acquisition max at x=3 with u=0.35; for t=3, μ=[0.0,0.4,-0.1,0.8,0.6], σ=[0.1,0.3,0.1,0.1,0.4], u=[0.02,0.10,0.03,0.05,0.25], observations at x=0,2,3 with newest at x=3, acquisition max at x=4 with u=0.25; for t=4, μ=[0.0,0.5,-0.1,0.8,0.3], σ=[0.1,0.2,0.1,0.1,0.1], u=[0.01,0.04,0.02,0.03,0.05], observations at x=0,2,3,4 with newest at x=4, acquisition max at x=4 with u=0.05. Add subtle arrow annotations exactly: in the t=2 subplot, arrow to one black observation labeled “observation (x)”, arrow to the black dashed curve labeled “objective fn f(x)”, and arrow below the green curve labeled “acquisition function u_t(x)”; in the t=3 subplot, arrow pointing to the red-outlined newest point labeled “new observation (x_t)”; in the t=4 subplot, arrow to the blue band labeled “posterior uncertainty μ_t(x) ± σ_t(x)” and arrow to the solid black curve labeled “posterior mean μ_t(x)”. Keep numeric readability by adding only a few value labels per stacked subplot: label the acquisition-max u value near the red triangle (e.g., “0.35”, “0.25”, “0.05”) and label the newest observation’s μ value near its marker in t=3 and t=4. Put a compact legend in the inset box inside (A) describing encodings: dashed black=Objective f(x), solid black=Posterior mean μ_t(x), blue band=Posterior uncertainty, green=Acquisition u_t(x), black dots=Observations, red-outlined dot=New observation, red inverted triangle=Acquisition max. Panel (B) (SUMMARY ROLE) shows a small comparison bar chart of acquisition maxima across t=2,3,4 using the same u-max values derived from (A): bars labeled t=2, t=3, t=4 with heights 0.35, 0.25, 0.05; place the x-category labels below and a y label “max u_t(x)”; add numeric labels on top of each bar (0.35, 0.25, 0.05) and a small note beneath the bars listing the argmax x values “x*=3,4,4” aligned under their respective bars. Panel (C) (DISTRIBUTION/DIAGNOSTIC ROLE) shows a heatmap-like matrix (rows t=2,3,4; columns x=0..4) for σ_t(x) values using the exact σ numbers above; include a small vertical grayscale or blue intensity key on the right side inside (C) and annotate only three cells with numbers for readability: at (t=2,x=4) “0.6”, at (t=3,x=1) “0.3”, at (t=4,x=1) “0.2”; keep row labels on the left and column labels along the bottom. Below the subtitle inside (A), arrange two equal-sized donut (ring) charts side by side: the left donut is Humans, the right donut is the Universe, with a prominent white circular hole in the center of each. To the left of the left donut inside (A), include a vertical legend with small colored squares and labels in this exact order and exact color associations: Oxygen (dark blue), Carbon (teal), Hydrogen (medium green), Nitrogen (pale yellow-green), Helium (light orange), Other (light gray). Humans donut slices must be exactly: Oxygen 61%, Carbon 23%, Hydrogen 10%, Nitrogen 2.6%, Helium 0%, Other 3.4%. Universe donut slices must be exactly: Hydrogen 75%, Helium 23%, Oxygen 1%, Carbon 0.5%, Nitrogen 0.1%, Other 0.4%. Ensure slice sizes match percentages and colors match the legend. Label every slice on both donuts with category name and percentage (e.g., "Oxygen 61%", "Hydrogen 75%"), placing text just inside or immediately outside with leader lines where needed; use white text on dark slices and dark text on light slices for readability. Under the left donut, center a bold caption "in humans"; under the right donut, center a bold caption "in the Universe" with "Universe" emphasized in bold relative to "in the". Add a non-trivial structural pattern: inset a small rounded-rectangle callout box inside the lower-right area of panel (A), partially overlapping the whitespace near the right donut but not covering any slice labels; the inset contains two short bullet highlights with big numbers only: "Humans: Oxygen 61%" and "Universe: Hydrogen 75%". Panel (B) (KPI summary strip): present four compact KPI tiles laid out in a single horizontal row within (B), aligned along their top edges; each KPI shows a short label and one large number derived from the same values: (i) "Top in Humans" = "Oxygen 61%"; (ii) "Top in Universe" = "Hydrogen 75%"; (iii) "Helium gap" = "+23% (Universe vs Humans)"; (iv) "Hydrogen gap" = "+65% (Universe vs Humans)". Use subtle outlines and keep numbers large; include only these four numeric readouts. Panel (C) (comparison view): create a grouped horizontal bar chart comparing Humans vs Universe for the six elements, ordered by Universe share descending (Hydrogen, Helium, Oxygen, Carbon, Nitrogen, Other). Use two bars per element (Humans and Universe) with clear legend keys at the top of (C); bars use neutral fills (not the element colors) to avoid confusion with (A), but the element names appear as left-side category labels. Add only a few value labels: label the Universe bar for Hydrogen as "75%", the Universe bar for Helium as "23%", and the Humans bar for Oxygen as "61%"; keep the rest unlabeled but scaled correctly. Panel (D) (Humans distribution focus): show a single stacked bar (or 100% stacked horizontal bar) for Humans only using the exact element colors from (A) and the same six categories; place the element labels directly on the largest segments only and include numeric labels only for "Oxygen 61%", "Carbon 23%", and "Hydrogen 10%"; add a small note near the bar end indicating the minor remainder without listing all numbers. Panel (E) (Universe distribution focus + diagnostic): show a single stacked bar (or 100% stacked horizontal bar) for Universe only using the exact element colors from (A); include numeric labels only for "Hydrogen 75%" and "Helium 23%" and one small label "Other elements (combined)" for the remainder. Add an overlapping callout badge in (E) that sits near the bar’s right side (overlapping the panel background but not the bar itself) stating "Everything else < 2%" to reflect Oxygen 1%, Carbon 0.5%, Nitrogen 0.1%, Other 0.4% combined; keep the badge anchored within (E) and ensure it does not protrude into (D). Maintain consistent typography across panels, crisp vector-like lines, high legibility, and keep all content contained within its panel boundaries.

prompt: Create a clean, high-resolution multi-panel dashboard on a white background using a modern sans-serif font like Arial, with a centered page title at the very top reading “Company Org Chart Dashboard”. Use a newspaper-style mosaic layout with seven labeled panels (A)–(G), placing each panel label in the top-left corner inside its panel. Do not include any data table in the image. Layout constraints (must all be satisfied): (1) Panel (A) is a tall anchor panel on the far left and spans the full height of the dashboard content area. (2) Panel (G) is a narrow vertical sidebar immediately to the right of (A) and runs vertically, separating the left anchor from the right block. (3) Panels (B) and (C) are in the upper-right block to the right of (G), stacked vertically with (B) above (C). (4) Panels (D), (E), and (F) form a single bottom row across the full width to the right of (A), placed below (G), below (B), and below (C). (5) The left edges of (B) and (C) align with the left edge of the right block (immediately right of (G)). (6) The right edges of (B), (C), (D), (E), and (F) align to a common right boundary. (7) The top edge of (A) aligns with the top edge of (B), and the bottom edge of (A) aligns with the bottom edge of the bottom-row panels (D)-(F). (8) Panels (D), (E), and (F) are equal-height and aligned along a shared baseline, and their top edges align with each other. (9) Add a non-trivial structural pattern: an inset callout card inside panel (A) that slightly overlaps the org chart content area in the lower-right corner of (A), containing a mini “Key totals” summary. Panel (A) (primary content): recreate the original organizational chart exactly as described: centered within (A) draw a vertical hierarchy of rounded rectangles connected by thin light-gray orthogonal lines; top center light-gray box “Shareholders” with smaller second line “Team size: 3”; directly below light-gray box “Board” with second line “Team size: 5”; below that a dark red box for CEO labeled two lines “Atle Sivertsen” and “CEO – Team size: 1”; under the CEO, a horizontal row of four equal medium-blue boxes connected vertically to the CEO, left-to-right: “Christer Vasseng” / “CTO – Team size: 8”, “Torstein Hønsi” / “CPO – Team size: 6”, “Anita Nesse” / “CSO – Team size: 5”, “Anne Jorunn Fjærestad” / “CFO – Team size: 4”; beneath executives, medium-green team boxes connected to the correct manager: under CTO one box “Product developers” / “Team – Team size: 10”; under CPO one box “Web devs, sys admin” / “Team – Team size: 7”; under CSO two boxes stacked vertically and centered under her with connector lines: upper “Sales team” / “Team – Team size: 6”, lower “Marketing team” / “Team – Team size: 5”; no team boxes under CFO; use white text in all colored boxes for contrast, keep box sizes consistent within each level, and ensure each team-size number is clearly readable. Inset in (A): the overlapping inset card reads “Key totals” and lists a few numbers only (no long lists): “Executives: 4”, “Teams: 4”, “Largest team: 10”. Panel (G) (narrow sidebar legend & hierarchy key): show a vertical legend with small rounded swatches and labels in order top-to-bottom: light gray “Governance”, dark red “CEO”, blue “Executives”, green “Teams”; beneath the legend add a short “Hierarchy notes” block with two bullet points and only two numeric callouts: “Depth: 4 levels” and “Direct reports to CEO: 4”. Panel (B) (upper-right, comparison chart): a horizontal bar chart titled “Leadership team sizes (people)” comparing CEO and the four executives, with bars ordered top-to-bottom: CEO 1, CTO 8, CPO 6, CSO 5, CFO 4; color CEO bar dark red and executive bars blue; place numeric value labels only at the end of each bar (1, 8, 6, 5, 4) and keep axis minimal. Panel (C) (below B, distribution view): a donut chart titled “Headcount by layer” with four segments using the same palette as the org chart: Governance (Shareholders+Board) total 8, CEO 1, Executives 23 (8+6+5+4), Teams 28 (10+7+6+5); place only four numeric labels near the donut segments (8, 1, 23, 28) and a small center label “Total shown: 60”. Panel (D) (bottom row left, KPI strip panel): a compact set of three large-number tiles titled “KPIs” arranged horizontally within (D): “Total headcount shown: 60”, “Largest single node: Product developers (10)”, “Smallest node: CEO (1)”; keep only these three numbers prominent. Panel (E) (bottom row middle, team comparison): a vertical bar chart titled “Team sizes (teams only)” with four green bars labeled “Product developers 10”, “Web devs, sys admin 7”, “Sales team 6”, “Marketing team 5”, with numeric labels above each bar (10, 7, 6, 5) and minimal gridlines. Panel (F) (bottom row right, diagnostic/structure slice): a simple node-link mini-map titled “Reporting fan-out” showing three stacked rows of labeled counts: top row “Governance nodes: 2”, middle row “Leadership nodes: 5” (CEO+4 execs), bottom row “Team nodes: 4”; connect rows with light gray lines and include only these three numeric labels (2, 5, 4). Maintain consistent typography, thin light-gray separators between panels, and ensure all labels remain readable without clutter.

label the upper subpanel in bold 'The plane is more expensive' and order cities top-to-bottom as Prague, Munich, Vienna, Amsterdam, Montpellier, Brussels, Lyon; for each city draw a horizontal dumbbell with green filled circle (train) on the left, purple filled circle (plane) on the right, thin connecting line, and a small black outline on each circle; place purple numeric difference labels just to the right of each plane circle showing plane minus train with exact values: Prague train 60€ plane 170€ +110€; Munich 50€ 146€ +96€; Vienna 80€ 170€ +90€; Amsterdam 80€ 130€ +50€; Montpellier 180€ 230€ +50€; Brussels 120€ 130€ +10€; Lyon 150€ 160€ +10€; label the lower subpanel in bold 'The plane is cheaper' and order cities top-to-bottom as Copenhagen, Bordeaux, Stockholm, Paris, Milan; Bordeaux 235€ 185€ −50€; Stockholm 140€ 80€ −60€; Paris 210€ 140€ −70€; Milan 190€ 70€ −120€; keep all points visually aligned to the shared x-axis scale; place a centered gray italic footnote at the bottom inside panel (A) reading 'Average prices come from a flight search site and a rail booking site.'; add a non-trivial structural element by placing a small inset callout inside panel (A) that overlaps the upper-right area of the dumbbell region without covering the title, showing a mini ranked list (tiny horizontal bars or lollipops) of the three largest absolute gaps labeled with values only: Milan 120€, Prague 110€, Munich 96€, with a thin border and a short caption 'Largest gaps'. Panel (D) (bottom-left card) should show a distribution view of prices: two side-by-side boxplots (or violin+dot overlay) for Train and Plane using the 12 values, with a few labeled dots for extremes only (Bordeaux train 235€, Milan plane 70€, Montpellier plane 230€); align its visual baseline and typography with other panels. Panel (E) (bottom-right card) should be a scatter diagnostic: x-axis Train price (€) and y-axis Plane price (€), include a thin gray diagonal reference line y=x, plot the 12 cities as small points (green outline with purple fill or two-tone marker), and label only three points with city name and one numeric pair each: Milan (190,70), Prague (60,170), Bordeaux (235,185); ensure all panels use consistent sans-serif typography, light gray gridlines where appropriate, and keep all text and numeric labels crisp and readable.

Panel (B) (below A) shows a ranked horizontal bar chart of the same four groups, sorted from highest to lowest, using the same colors; place category labels on the left, bars extending right, and place only two numeric labels: 76.5% on the White bar and 6.9% on the Asian bar; keep the bar chart title small and left-aligned near the top of panel (B) reading “Ranked shares”. prompt: Create a clean, white-background, sans-serif multi-panel scientific dashboard following the layout contract seven_hub_spokes_with_bands, with each panel labeled in bold “(A)” through “(G)” placed consistently at the top-left inside its panel. Do not include any data table in the image. Spatial layout constraints (must all be satisfied): (1) Panel (A) is the central hub. (2) Panel (B) is directly above (A) as the top satellite, horizontally centered with (A). (3) Panel (C) is directly left of (A) as the left satellite, vertically centered with (A). (4) Panel (D) is directly right of (A) as the right satellite, vertically centered with (A). (5) Panel (E) is directly below (A) as the bottom satellite, horizontally centered with (A). (6) Panel (F) is a full-width top band spanning across the entire dashboard, positioned above (B) and above (A), acting as the header band. (7) Panel (G) is a full-width bottom band spanning across the entire dashboard, positioned below (E) and below (A), acting as the footer band. (8) Align the left edge of (C) with the left edge of the spanning band (F), and align the right edge of (D) with the right edge of the spanning band (F); also keep the horizontal centerline of (B), (A), and (E) aligned. Add a non-trivial structural pattern: include a semi-transparent inset callout panel inside (A) that overlaps the plotted area, anchored in the upper-left of (A), containing a compact “dominant gas by height band” mini-strip with labeled altitude bands. Panel (F) header band: include the main title “Atmospheric composition by height” in bold black and directly beneath it a smaller gray subtitle “Source: Online reference”; also include a compact KPI row in (F) with five small colored swatches and short labels for N2, O2, O, He, H (no large legend here), plus two numeric callouts such as “At 0 km: N2 78%, O2 21%” and “At 1000 km: H 70%, He 25%”. Panel (A) primary chart (must match the original chart content): a tall stacked area chart of atmospheric composition versus height with light gray gridlines; x-axis along the bottom labeled “Volume fraction (%)” from 0% at left to 100% at right with ticks 0%, 25%, 50%, 75%, 100%; y-axis on the right labeled “Height (km)” from 0 at bottom to 1000 at top with ticks 0, 200, 400, 600, 800, 1000. Plot five smoothly interpolated horizontal stacked areas whose widths at each listed height correspond exactly to these percentages and sum to 100%: at 0 km: N2 78, O2 21, O 1, He 0, H 0; at 200 km: N2 40, O2 10, O 45, He 4, H 1; at 400 km: N2 10, O2 2, O 60, He 20, H 8; at 600 km: N2 2, O2 1, O 40, He 35, H 22; at 800 km: N2 0, O2 0, O 20, He 35, H 45; at 1000 km: N2 0, O2 0, O 5, He 25, H 70. Stack order from left to right at every height: N2, O2, O, He, H. Colors: N2 dark purple, O2 rich medium blue, O slightly teal blue, He light cyan, H pale green. Place bold gas symbols centered inside their regions at appropriate mid-heights (ensure readable): “N2”, “O2”, “O”, “He”, “H”. Add two pale vertical background bands: one behind Sep 2020 to Jan 2021 and another behind Jun 2022 to Aug 2022. Add four annotation callouts with arrows and triangular bullets placed so they do not cover bar labels: left side above the Sep 2020–Jan 2021 band with a black triangular bullet and text “Sept 2020–Jan 2021, I paid for a yearly subscription (monthly cost: €63.42, which is €34.40 more compared with the €29 ticket). I often spent more, for example to take bikes, friends, or to travel farther out of the city.”; near the negative cluster Feb 2021–Jun 2021 with a downward-pointing triangular bullet and text “The five months after canceling my subscription, I spent on average €12.40 on public transport (€16.60 less than the €29 ticket).”; near Aug 2021 with a left-pointing triangular bullet and text “In August 2021, family and friends visited and stayed on the other side of the city, so I took public transport a lot. prompt: Create a single high-resolution scientific infographic/dashboard on a clean white background with light gray gridlines where specified, thin black axes, and all text in a LaTeX-like serif font (Times New Roman style). Do not include any data table in the image. Panel (D) (upper-right) is a derived diagnostic for Prompt A: a single clean line+marker chart titled “Stability across layers (derived from A)” with x-axis “Model family” showing four categories A,B,C,D and y-axis “Drop after peak (Correlation)” from 0.00 to 0.60; for each family compute and plot the largest drop from that family’s best model peak to its final layer value (use labeled points): Family A drop 0.04 (peak 0.72 to final 0.68 for Model A-large), Family B drop 0.42 (peak 0.72 to final 0.30 for Model B-large), Family C drop 0.48 (peak 0.68 to final 0.20 for Model C-large), Family D drop 0.34 (peak 0.52 to final 0.18 for Model D-small); label each marker with the drop value. Panel (E) (lower-left) is a derived comparison for Prompt B: a compact bar+arrow panel titled “Encoder D vs E deltas (derived from B)” with two sections stacked vertically inside (E): top section “Benchmark X gaps” shows two diverging bars on a zero-centered axis labeled “D − E (percentage points)”, one for Alignment +19 and one for Fidelity +5, each labeled; bottom section “Pareto best-at-CLIP=0.25” shows two labeled points at CLIP 0.25 with FID values D=11 and E=11 side-by-side on a tiny axis (or a mini callout) indicating a tie, with exact numeric labels. Keep all typography consistent, ensure all legends remain inside their respective plotting areas, and ensure numeric labels are readable without crowding by using small but crisp text and slight offsets from marks.

prompt: Create a single high-resolution scientific infographic/dashboard on a pure white canvas using thin black panel borders and ample whitespace; follow the layout contract four_context_top_compare_bottom with exactly four labeled panels: (C) is a shallow context band spanning the full width across the very top edge, (A) and (B) are the two primary source panels placed side-by-side in the middle tier with equal widths and perfectly aligned top and bottom edges, and (D) is a comparison band spanning the full width across the very bottom edge; keep consistent outer margins on all sides and use a uniform vertical gutter between (C) and the middle tier, and another uniform vertical gutter between the middle tier and (D), plus a single narrow vertical gutter between (A) and (B). Place each panel label exactly as “(A) (B) (C) (D)” in bold at the top-left inside its panel border, with identical offset padding from the panel’s top and left. Do not include any data table in the image. Panel (A) (left middle) must contain three vertically stacked line-chart subpanels inside it (top/middle/bottom) with equal heights, identical inner left and right margins, and perfectly aligned x-axes so the tick marks vertically line up across the three subpanels; each subpanel uses thin black axes, no gridlines, x-axis labeled “Input x” ranging 0 to 4 with ticks at 0,1,2,3,4, and y-axis labeled “Function value” ranging about -0.5 to 1.0 with ticks at -0.5,0.0,0.5,1.0; centered above each subpanel place italic serif titles “t = 2” (top), “t = 3” (middle), “t = 4” (bottom). In every (A) subpanel, plot (1) the objective f(x) as a black dashed smooth curve through the discrete points at x=0..4, (2) the posterior mean μ_t(x) as a black solid smooth curve through its discrete points, (3) a semi-transparent soft blue uncertainty band for μ_t(x) ± σ_t(x), and (4) a thin green curve for acquisition u_t(x) near the bottom of the plotting area; show observations as solid black filled circles at the specified x locations, and in subpanels where a new observation is introduced, highlight the newest one as a white-filled circle with a red outline; on the green curve place a small red inverted triangle at the x where u_t(x) is maximal and annotate with a small arrow and italic text “acquisition max”. Use these exact numbers to shape the curves (smooth interpolation between x=0,1,2,3,4) and also place small numeric labels directly on key marks: label each red inverted triangle with “u=” value and each new observation with “f=” value (compact, readable): for t=2 use f: [x0 0.0, x1 0.6, x2 -0.2, x3 0.8, x4 0.3], μ: [0.0,0.3,-0.2,0.4,0.5], σ: [0.1,0.4,0.1,0.5,0.6], u: [0.05,0.20,0.05,0.35,0.30], observations at x=0 and x=2; acquisition max at x=3 with u=0.35; for t=3 use f: [0.0,0.6,-0.2,0.8,0.3], μ: [0.0,0.4,-0.1,0.8,0.6], σ: [0.1,0.3,0.1,0.1,0.4], u: [0.02,0.10,0.03,0.05,0.25], observations at x=0,x=2,x=3 with the x=3 point marked as the new observation; acquisition max at x=4 with u=0.25; for t=4 use f: [0.0,0.6,-0.2,0.8,0.3], μ: [0.0,0.5,-0.1,0.8,0.3], σ: [0.1,0.2,0.1,0.1,0.1], u: [0.01,0.04,0.02,0.03,0.05], observations at x=0,x=2,x=3,x=4 with the x=4 point marked as the new observation; acquisition max at x=4 with u=0.05. Add the specific arrow annotations inside (A): in t=2 subpanel, arrow to one black observation with label “observation (x)”, arrow to the dashed curve labeled “objective fn f(x)”, and arrow below the green curve labeled “acquisition function u_t(x)”; in t=3 subpanel, arrow to the red-outlined new point labeled “new observation (x_t)”; in t=4 subpanel, arrow to the blue band labeled “posterior uncertainty μ_t(x) ± σ_t(x)” and arrow to the solid black curve labeled “posterior mean μ_t(x)”. Include a small legend for (A) encodings placed inside (A) but outside the plotting areas, aligned to the right edge of (A) and vertically centered between the middle and bottom subpanels, mapping: dashed black=Objective f(x), solid black=Posterior mean μ_t(x), blue band=Posterior uncertainty, green line=Acquisition function u_t(x), black filled circles=Observations, red-outlined circle=New observation, red inverted triangle=Acquisition max. Panel (B) (right middle) must be a single plotting panel multi-series line chart with thin black axes and light gray dotted gridlines at major ticks; title centered above the plot reads “Accuracy Loss vs. Model Size Ratio after Compression”; x-axis label “Model Size Ratio after Compression (%)” with range 2% to 20% and major ticks at 2%,5%,8%,11%,14%,17%,20%; y-axis label “Accuracy Loss (%)” with range -4.5% to +0.5% and major ticks every 0.5% with horizontal dotted gridlines; plot four smooth solid lines with distinct markers and colors and a legend centered above the plot area (inside panel (B), directly under the title): Pruning + Quantization = red line with hollow circular markers at (2,-4.0),(3,-0.8),(4,-0.2),(5,-0.1),(6,-0.1),(7,-0.05),(8,0.0); Pruning Only = purple line with upward triangle markers at (5,-4.5),(6,-2.0),(7,-1.0),(8,-0.5),(11,-0.2),(14,0.0); Quantization Only = golden yellow line with square markers at (5,-3.0),(6,-1.8),(7,-1.2),(8,-0.8),(11,-0.4),(14,-0.3),(17,-0.2),(20,-0.1); SVD = green line with diamond markers at (14,-4.5),(17,-2.5),(20,-1.5). Ensure markers are clearly visible; red circles hollow, purple triangles hollow, yellow squares and green diamonds may be filled. Place small numeric labels adjacent to each marker showing the y-value (e.g., “-0.8%”) so values are visually checkable without any tabular listing, and keep label placement consistent (slightly above-right for most points, adjust locally to avoid overlap). Panel (C) (top context band) must visually bridge the two middle panels: place a concise dashboard header centered in (C) reading “Bayesian Optimization Iterations vs. Compression Tradeoffs” in clean sans-serif; beneath that header, split (C) into two aligned legend/text blocks: the left block is horizontally aligned with the left edge of panel (A) and summarizes the meaning of f(x), μ_t(x), σ_t(x), and u_t(x) with miniature swatches matching (A)’s styles; the right block is horizontally aligned with the right edge of panel (B) and summarizes the four compression methods with miniature line+marker swatches matching (B); keep both blocks on the same baseline so (C) explicitly connects (A) and (B) as parallel stories. Panel (D) (bottom comparison band) must synthesize both charts and be split into two derived mini-panels within (D): a left mini-plot positioned directly under panel (A) and a right mini-plot positioned directly under panel (B), separated by a thin vertical divider centered exactly beneath the gutter between (A) and (B). In (D-left), create a compact lollipop or line plot titled “Acquisition-max trajectory” showing t on the x-axis (ticks at 2,3,4) and the acquisition-max x* on the y-axis (ticks at 0,1,2,3,4), with three labeled points: (t=2,x*=3) label “u*=0.35”, (t=3,x*=4) label “u*=0.25”, (t=4,x*=4) label “u*=0.05”; in (D-right), create a compact bar chart titled “Best (closest-to-zero) accuracy loss by method” with one bar per method using the best (maximum) accuracy-loss value from panel (B)’s series and label each bar tip with the exact value: Pruning + Quantization best = 0.0% (at 8%), Pruning Only best = 0.0% (at 14%), Quantization Only best = -0.1% (at 20%), SVD best = -1.5% (at 20%); order bars left-to-right to match the legend order in (B). Cross-panel structural relation requirement: in (D), add a small centered annotation above the vertical divider reading “Synthesis: exploration (u*) vs. performance loss” with two thin arrows pointing upward—left arrow pointing to panel (A)’s green acquisition curves, right arrow pointing to panel (B)’s lines—so (D) explicitly references and compares both source panels. Use clean sans-serif for axes/ticks across the dashboard, keep panel (A)’s subpanel titles in italic serif as specified, and ensure all plotted numbers and labels remain sharp and readable.

prompt: Create a high-resolution, clean scientific infographic/dashboard on a pure white background in a modern sans-serif style, divided into six clearly separated panels labeled (A), (B), (C), (D), (E), (F) with each panel label placed in the top-left corner of its panel. Layout contract six_center_spine_split_sides: arrange the top row as three primary columns with panel (A) on the left, panel (C) as a tall narrow vertical center comparison spine, and panel (B) on the right; panels (D), (E), (F) must form a bottom row directly beneath (A), (C), (B) respectively, aligned to the same three columns. Spatial constraints (must all be satisfied): (1) Panels (A), (C), (B) share the same top edge and the same height; (2) panel (C) is centered horizontally on the full canvas and is narrower than (A) and (B); (3) panels (A) and (B) are equal width and symmetrically placed to the left and right of (C) with equal horizontal gutter spacing; (4) panels (D), (E), (F) share the same bottom edge, are equal height to each other, and sit directly beneath their corresponding top panels (D under A, E under C, F under B) with perfectly aligned left/right edges to their column; (5) all six panels have consistent margins and thin light-gray divider lines between panels; (6) panel (C) must visually bridge panels (A) and (B) by including two thin horizontal connector arrows at mid-height pointing left into (A) and right into (B); (7) panel (E) must reference both top-row topics by placing two small header tags inside (E) aligned to its top edge, one on the left reading “Beam displacement” and one on the right reading “Element mass shares”; (8) the bottom-row panels (D)(E)(F) must align vertically so their titles sit on the same baseline; (9) inside panel (B), the two donuts must be equal diameter and aligned on the same horizontal centerline. Do not include any data table in the image. Panel (A) content (source chart 1): within panel (A), place three equal-sized 3D surface plots of the same rectangular cantilever beam in a single horizontal row with small gaps; label above each sub-plot (within panel A, centered over each beam) as “Case 1”, “Case 2”, “Case 3”. In every Case, render the long beam with a slight perspective tilt so the front-right corner is closer to the viewer, and map scalar displacement to the beam surface with a smooth rainbow colormap from deep blue (minimum) through green/yellow to bright red (maximum). In each Case sub-plot, place a small 3-axis orientation triad near the lower area of that sub-plot (not overlapping the beam), drawn as three short arrows colored red, green, and yellow. For Case 1: add a slim vertical colorbar immediately to the left of the beam (inside the Case 1 sub-plot area), with tick labels exactly 0.0, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5 (bottom to top) and put the unit label “(cm)” just to the right of that colorbar; ensure beam colors progress from blue near the fixed end to red near the free end consistent with those values. For Case 2: repeat the same orientation, add a separate slim vertical colorbar immediately to the left of the beam with the same tick labels 0.0, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5 and the unit label “(cm)” to the right of the colorbar, but make the red region smaller/less intense to reflect smaller displacements. For Case 3: render smoother wave-like color variation; add a vertical colorbar immediately to the left of the beam with tick labels exactly 1.1e-17, 2.0e-4, 4.0e-4, 6.0e-4, 9.4e-4 (bottom to top) and do not place any unit text next to this colorbar; keep the same blue-to-red meaning. Panel (B) content (source chart 2): inside panel (B), at the very top center place a bold black sans-serif title “Hydrogen makes up three quarters of the mass of the Universe”, and directly beneath it a smaller regular-weight subtitle “Share of chemical elements in humans vs. in the Universe, by mass”. Below the subtitle, place two large donut (ring) charts side by side: left donut = humans, right donut = Universe; keep both donuts the same size and aligned horizontally. To the left of the left donut, place a vertical legend with small colored squares and labels in this exact order and color mapping: Oxygen (dark blue), Carbon (teal), Hydrogen (medium green), Nitrogen (pale yellow-green), Helium (light orange), Other (light gray). Each donut must have a prominent white circular hole. Humans donut slices and labels (each slice labeled with name + percentage, placed inside the slice or just outside with leader lines as needed, using white text on dark slices and dark text on light slices): Oxygen 61%, Carbon 23%, Hydrogen 10%, Nitrogen 2.6%, Helium 0%, Other 3.4%. Universe donut slices and labels: Hydrogen 75%, Helium 23%, Oxygen 1%, Carbon 0.5%, Nitrogen 0.1%, Other 0.4%. Under the left donut center a bold caption “in humans”; under the right donut center a bold caption “in the Universe” with “Universe” emphasized in heavier bold. Panel (C) content (central vertical comparison spine): make panel (C) a tall, narrow, two-section vertical spine with a thin horizontal divider line halfway down; the top section titled “Scale contrast” and the bottom section titled “Dominant component contrast”, both titles centered within (C). In the top section, draw two stacked mini vertical gauge bars: left gauge labeled “Beam cases 1–2 max 1.5 cm” and right gauge labeled “Beam case 3 max 9.4e-4”, with the numeric maxima written large and the min values shown small at the bottom (“0.0” for cases 1–2 and “1.1e-17” for case 3); keep gauges aligned so their bottoms line up, emphasizing the magnitude difference. In the bottom section, show two large callouts side by side: “Humans: Oxygen 61%” (colored with the Oxygen dark blue swatch) and “Universe: Hydrogen 75%” (colored with the Hydrogen medium green swatch), each with the percentage in large type; place these callouts so their centers align vertically with the connector arrows that point into panels (A) and (B). Panel (D) derived panel (bottom-left, beneath A): create a clean 2D line chart titled “Displacement along beam (labeled points)” with x-axis “Position (cm)” and y-axis “Displacement”; plot three lines with circular markers for Case 1, Case 2, Case 3 using distinct colors and a small legend in the top-right of panel (D). prompt: Create a clean, modern infographic-style single-panel line chart on a white background about charitable donations in Germany over time. Title at the top in bold black sans-serif: "In Germany, fewer people donate, but more money gets donated". Add a multi-clause subtitle beneath in smaller black sans-serif: "Percentage change in donated money and in the number of donating people from 2014–2020, using 2014 as the baseline, and highlighting how the two trends diverge after 2015." Do not include any data table in the image. Use thin black axes and light gray horizontal gridlines. X-axis label: "Year" with tick labels exactly: 2014, 2015, 2016, 2017, 2018, 2019, 2020. Y-axis label: "Percentage change vs 2014 (%)" with tick labels exactly: -20, -15, -10, -5, 0, +5, +10, +15, +20. Plot two teal-toned line series with circular markers, using the exact values (percentage change vs 2014): Donated money (%) by year: 2014=0, 2015=11, 2016=6, 2017=5, 2018=7, 2019=4, 2020=9; Number of people who donated (%) by year: 2014=0, 2015=2, 2016=-1, 2017=-4, 2018=-7, 2019=-11, 2020=-14. Keep the upper line as "donated money" (generally above zero after 2014) and the lower line as "number of people who donated" (trending downward into negatives). Place small, readable numeric value labels next to every marker on both lines with exact formatting including signs and percent symbols (e.g., "11%", "-11%", "0%", "+?" not allowed—use the exact numbers; include minus signs where needed). Add series end labels near the rightmost ends in teal text: "donated money" next to the upper 2020 point and "number of people who donated" next to the lower 2020 point. Add a compact legend box in the upper-left of the plot area with two line+marker swatches and labels exactly: "donated money" and "number of people who donated"; directly under the legend, add a legend note in small gray sans-serif (1–2 sentences): "How to read: values show change relative to 2014 (0%). Positive percentages indicate growth vs 2014; negative percentages indicate decline vs 2014." Add a vertical light gray shaded band spanning the plot area between the x-axis positions for 2019 and 2020; at the top of this band write "change in 2020" in black sans-serif. Inside the shaded band, next to the upper line, add a bold teal annotation reading "donated money +5.1%" and next to the lower line add a bold teal annotation reading "number of people who donated -2.6%" (these are textual highlights only and must not alter the plotted point values). Add at least two additional callout annotations with leader lines: (1) near the 2015 point on the upper series, a teal callout reading "2015 spike: donated money reaches 11%"; (2) near the 2020 point on the lower series, a teal callout reading "By 2020: number of people who donated falls to -14%"; optionally add a third smaller gray callout near 2019 on the lower series reading "2019: -11% marks the steepening decline". Include a short footnote centered below the chart in small gray text: "Source note: values are indexed to 2014 (baseline = 0%)." Ensure all text (title, subtitle, axis labels, ticks, legend labels, legend note, callouts, annotations, footnote, and all value labels) is crisp, correctly spelled, and highly legible in a minimalist sans-serif style, with teal lines/annotations and light gray gridlines.

prompt: Create a 3-panel multi-panel scientific figure on a white background with thin light-gray panel borders, arranged as: large panel (A) on the left, and on the right a vertical stack with panel (B) above panel (C); place the panel labels “(A)”, “(B)”, “(C)” in the top-left corner inside each panel in bold black text. Panel (A): a single workflow diagram titled and labeled as follows: at the top center, a dark blue horizontal rounded rectangle labeled “Question” in centered white sans-serif text; from its bottom center draw a solid black downward arrow into the main body. Near the top-left of the main body, place the bold black title “Causal Reasoning Chain Model.” On the left side, create a tall vertical blue rounded rectangle labeled “Preparation Phase” in white text; immediately to its right, stack four pastel rounded rectangles vertically, aligned in a column: Step 1 pale yellow with small bold “Step 1” at upper-left and text “Extract the causal graph.”; Step 2 light blue with “Step 2” and “Determine the query type.”; Step 3 light pink with “Step 3” and “Formalize the query.”; Step 4 light green with “Step 4” and “Gather all relevant data.” Beneath these four boxes, draw a black curly brace spanning the full width of the stacked boxes, with centered caption below it: “Inputs to causal estimation.” Below the Preparation Phase block on the left, place another tall vertical blue rounded rectangle labeled “Solution Phase” in white text; immediately to its right, stack two rounded rectangles aligned vertically: Step 5 light purple with “Step 5” and “Deduce the estimand using causal inference and do-calculus.” above Step 6 pale orange with “Step 6” and “Calculate the estimand using data and basic arithmetic.” Draw a short solid black downward arrow from the bottom of Step 5 to the top of Step 6. In the upper middle-right of panel (A), to the right of the Preparation steps column, draw a small causal graph: three white circles with thin black borders labeled “X”, “Y”, “Z” in black, arranged in a triangle, with solid black arrows Z→X, Z→Y, and X→Y. Immediately to the right of the graph, place a formula block: bold heading “Average Treatment Effect (ATE)”, then the equation “ATE = E[Y|do(X=1)] - E[Y|do(X=0)]”, then three short lines beneath: “P(Z=1)=0.50, P(Z=0)=0.50”; “P(Y=1|X=1,Z=0)=0.08, P(Y=1|X=0,Z=0)=0.04”; “P(Y=1|X=1,Z=1)=0.09, P(Y=1|X=0,Z=1)=0.06”. From the bottom of the Step 6 box, draw a solid black downward arrow to a centered math expression below the Solution Phase area: “ATE(X) = Σ_z P(Z=z)[P(Y=1|X=1,Z=z) - P(Y=1|X=0,Z=z)]”, and directly below it add “ATE(X) = -0.021 < 0”. At the bottom of panel (A), add a dark blue horizontal rounded rectangle spanning most of the panel width; on its left write “Final Q” in white sans-serif text, and to the right of that within the same bottom band place the short instruction text: “Output one word: Yes or No.” Centered just below this bottom band, add bold dark blue text: “Final answer: No”. Panel (B): a compact “Graph + Query” summary derived from the same topic, with the same causal graph (Z→X, Z→Y, X→Y) placed on the left and, aligned to its right, a small text block containing only the bold heading “ATE” and the equation “ATE = E[Y|do(X=1)] - E[Y|do(X=0)]”; add a single solid black arrow pointing from the graph to the equation block. Panel (C): a compact “Steps-only” ablation derived from the same topic, showing a simplified vertical flow centered in the panel: three small rounded rectangles stacked top-to-bottom with solid black downward arrows between them, labeled “Prep”, “Do-calculus”, “Compute”; under the stack, place the short result line “ATE < 0 ⇒ No”; ensure all arrows are solid black, all text is readable, alignment is tidy, and no logos or external branding appear.

prompt: Create a clean academic 3-panel scientific figure on a white background with three panels arranged in a single horizontal row: (A) on the left, (B) in the center, (C) on the right; place the panel labels “(A)”, “(B)”, “(C)” in the top-left corner inside each panel in bold sans-serif. Use consistent sans-serif typography, thin dark-gray outlines, crisp arrows, and short text strings. Panel (A) (main content) shows an architecture diagram explaining three ways of enforcing logical relations between events for a language model: at the very top-left of panel (A), place a small black person silhouette icon with the bold label “User” immediately to its right; to the right of this user label and aligned with it near the top-center of panel (A), draw a wide light-blue rounded rectangle with a thin dark-gray border containing two stacked text lines: “Instruction: Given a text with two events, identify four types of relations between them.” above “Text: A large FIRE broke out at a city supermarket on Main Street, and the entrance roof COLLAPSED during the blaze.”; below this blue rectangle, align three tall rounded-rectangle modules in a row (left/middle/right), equal width and vertically aligned at their tops, each with a thin dark-gray outline and a pale fill: left module pale green titled “LLM Reasoning”, middle module pale orange titled “Logical Rule Set”, right module pale yellow titled “LLM-LR Dataset”; from the bottom edge of the blue rectangle, draw three dashed dark-gray arrows downward, each arrow pointing to the top-center of one of the three colored modules (dashed arrows only here). Inside the left pale green module, stack elements top-to-bottom: a light box labeled “LLM Thought” containing the short sentence “FIRE may CAUSE COLLAPSE; events can OVERLAP.”; below it a dashed-border box titled “Logical Constraints” listing exactly four left-aligned lines: “Coreference: FALSE”, “Temporal: OVERLAP”, “Causal: CAUSE”, “Subevent: NONE”; below that place the caption “Output answers” and directly beneath it a small green circle with a white check mark. Inside the middle pale orange module, stack elements: a light box labeled “Rule examples” with two numbered lines: “(1) COREFERENCE -> no TEMPORAL/CAUSAL/SUBEVENT.” and “(2) CAUSE -> BEFORE or OVERLAP.”; below it a dashed-border box titled “Original answers” with four lines: “Coreference: TRUE”, “Temporal: SIMULTANEOUS”, “Causal: CAUSE”, “Subevent: NONE”, and place a small red circle with a white exclamation mark immediately next to the word “SIMULTANEOUS”; from the bottom-center of this “Original answers” box draw a solid dark-gray downward arrow to a second dashed-border box titled “Conflict-free answers” listing exactly four lines: “Coreference: FALSE”, “Temporal: OVERLAP”, “Causal: CAUSE”, “Subevent: NONE”; below it place the caption “Conflict-free answers” and beneath that a small green circle with a white check mark. Inside the right pale yellow module, stack elements: a light box labeled “Example training instance” containing three short lines: “Context: A~B COREFERENCE; B~C COREFERENCE.”, “Question: relations A vs C?”, “Answers: COREFERENCE=FALSE; TEMPORAL=OVERLAP; CAUSAL=CAUSE; SUBEVENT=NONE.”; below this box draw a solid downward arrow to a horizontally oriented rounded rectangle labeled “Specialized LLM”; below that draw a solid downward arrow to a dashed-border box titled “Generated answers” listing exactly four lines: “Coreference: FALSE”, “Temporal: OVERLAP”, “Causal: CAUSE”, “Subevent: NONE”; directly below the dashed box place a small green circle with a white check mark. Panel (B) is a compact ablation/simplification view aligned with panel (A): depict only the same three colored modules (pale green, pale orange, pale yellow) as smaller rounded rectangles arranged left-to-right, with their titles centered at top; above them place a small light-blue rounded rectangle labeled “Input (Instruction + Text)”; connect this input to each module using three dashed downward arrows aligned to each module’s center; inside each module include only one small dashed-border box labeled “4 relations” listing the four short tokens on one line each: “Coreference”, “Temporal”, “Causal”, “Subevent” (no TRUE/FALSE values), and show no other internal boxes or icons; keep all three modules vertically aligned and evenly spaced, emphasizing alignment and containment. Panel (C) is a compact qualitative comparison focusing on connectivity and grouping: create a two-row layout inside panel (C) with a top row labeled “Before constraints” and a bottom row labeled “After constraints”, each row containing three small rounded rectangles aligned left-to-right labeled “Generative”, “Retrieval”, “Pretraining”; in the top row, place a tiny red conflict marker (red circle with white “!”) near the “Retrieval” box only; draw thin solid arrows from each top-row box straight down to the corresponding bottom-row box, aligned vertically; inside each bottom-row box place a tiny green check icon (green circle with white check) centered; ensure the three columns are vertically aligned across rows and the arrows are straight and parallel.
