prompt: Create a clean academic vector-style multi-panel scientific figure on a white background with three panels arranged in a two-column layout: a large panel (A) on the left spanning the full height, and two compact panels on the right stacked vertically with (B) above (C); place panel labels '(A)', '(B)', '(C)' in the top-left corner inside each panel in the same sans-serif font. Panel (A): Recreate an architecture-style reasoning pipeline diagram. At the very top of panel (A), place a wide light-gray rectangular header box with bold black text: 'Claim: Both Person A and the director of Film X were born in Country Y.' Below the header, arrange three vertical regions aligned left-to-right: (1) Left region: a rectangular box labeled 'Language Model' with a smaller stacked-paper shaped box labeled 'Exemplars' directly beneath it; draw a short downward arrow from the header box to the 'Language Model' box. (2) Center region: a large rounded rectangle centered vertically labeled along its top edge 'Reasoning Pipeline'; from the right side of 'Language Model' draw a solid black arrow into this rounded rectangle. Inside the 'Reasoning Pipeline' container, stack four horizontal colored step bars from top to bottom with clear separation; each bar has a small white circle with thin black outline overlapping its left edge containing the step id text: 'S1', 'S2', 'S3', 'S4' (top to bottom). Color S1 and S3 light blue, S2 light pink, S4 light green. Put inside each bar the exact short texts: S1 'Verify [Person A was born in Country Y.]', S2 'Question [Who is the director of Film X?]', S3 'Verify [{ANSWER_1} was born in Country Y.]', S4 'Predict [{FACT_1} AND {FACT_2}]'. To the immediate right of each corresponding bar (still inside the center region, not inside the bar), place aligned annotations: next to S1 'FACT_1 = TRUE' with 'TRUE' colored green; next to S2 'ANSWER_1 = Director B' in dark gray; next to S3 'FACT_2 = FALSE' with 'FALSE' colored red; next to S4 'PREDICTED_LABEL = REFUTES' with 'REFUTES' in uppercase black, and immediately beside it a red circular icon containing a white 'X'. Connect the bars with solid black downward arrows from S1 to S2, S2 to S3, and S3 to S4. (3) Right region: to the right of the 'Reasoning Pipeline' container, place a rounded-rectangle column titled 'Tools' at its top; inside it, place four stacked rectangular tool boxes vertically aligned with S1–S4 and labeled top-to-bottom: 'Fact Checker', 'QA Module', 'Fact Checker', 'Logic Engine'; from the center of each step bar draw a solid black horizontal arrow pointing right to the corresponding tool box. Further right, place a narrow rounded-rectangle column titled 'Knowledge Sources' at its top; inside it stack three boxes labeled 'Evidence DB', 'Open-book Corpus', 'Closed-book Model' (top-to-bottom). From each tool box, draw a double-headed horizontal arrow reaching to the center of the 'Knowledge Sources' column. Use thin black outlines, crisp arrows, and a consistent sans-serif font; ensure no text overlaps borders or arrows. Panel (B): A compact ablation view titled 'No Tools' placed near the top of panel (B); show a simplified flow left-to-right: a small box 'Claim' on the left feeding by a single arrow into a rounded rectangle 'Language Model Only' centered, then an arrow to an output box on the right labeled 'PREDICTED_LABEL'; beneath the output box place a small red circular icon with white 'X'; keep all elements aligned on one horizontal line with short labels. Panel (C): A compact module breakdown titled 'Tool Chain' near the top of panel (C); draw a single horizontal chain of four small boxes centered in the panel labeled 'Fact Checker', 'QA Module', 'Fact Checker', 'Logic Engine' connected by arrows left-to-right; below this chain, centered, place a narrow rounded rectangle labeled 'Knowledge Sources' with three tiny stacked sub-boxes inside labeled 'Evidence DB', 'Open-book Corpus', 'Closed-book Model'; draw short double-headed arrows between the bottom of each tool box and the top of 'Knowledge Sources' to indicate bidirectional access; keep the layout symmetric and avoid any long text.

prompt: Create a 3-panel multi-panel scientific figure on a white background in a clean academic style with crisp black vector lines and a sans-serif font; arrange three panels in a single row left-to-right: (A) on the left, (B) in the center, (C) on the right, with panel labels '(A)', '(B)', '(C)' in the top-left corner of each panel. Inside the dashed border at the top, draw a wide pale-yellow rounded rectangle containing 'Claim: Model A's accuracy of 62% is 12% higher than random guessing.' Directly below the claim, place two rounded rectangles aligned horizontally: left pale-orange box labeled 'Closed-domain knowledge: Evaluation table' and right pale-blue box labeled 'Commonsense knowledge'; connect the claim to the left knowledge box with a straight orange downward arrow and to the right knowledge box with a straight blue downward arrow. From the left knowledge box, draw a vertical chain of three rounded rectangles stacked downward, connected by black downward arrows: a white/light-gray box labeled 'Accuracy corresponds to Acc. column', then a pale-purple box labeled 'Simple lookup', then a white box labeled 'Model A's accuracy is 62%'. From the right pale-blue knowledge box, draw a black downward arrow to a white rounded rectangle labeled 'Random guessing accuracy is 50%'. Slightly below and between the two numeric statement boxes, draw a central pale-red rounded rectangle labeled 'Subtraction'; draw two black diagonal arrows into it, one from 'Model A's accuracy is 62%' and one from 'Random guessing accuracy is 50%'. Directly below 'Subtraction', add a white rounded rectangle stating 'The difference between 62% and 50% is 12%.' and connect with a black downward arrow. At the bottom center, draw a pale-green rounded rectangle labeled 'Fact checker: Supported' with a green check-mark icon immediately to its right; connect the difference statement to this verdict with a black downward arrow. In the bottom-right corner inside the dashed border, add a small white legend box with a thin solid black border containing two short lines: 'Red = numeric operation' and 'Green = final verdict'. Panel (B): a compact ablation view derived from the same topic, showing only the numeric reasoning core; place two white rounded rectangles side-by-side near the top labeled '62%' (left) and '50%' (right), both feeding via black diagonal arrows into a central pale-red rounded rectangle labeled 'Subtraction', then a black downward arrow to a white rounded rectangle labeled '12%', then a black downward arrow to a pale-green rounded rectangle labeled 'Supported' with a green check-mark icon to its right; keep the chain vertically centered and aligned. prompt: Create a clean vector-style multi-panel scientific figure on a white background arranged as three panels in a single row: (A) on the left, (B) in the middle, (C) on the right; place panel labels '(A)', '(B)', '(C)' in the top-left corner of each panel in bold black sans-serif. From the right edges of the three white rectangles, draw arrows converging into a cyan rectangle labeled 'MLP Head'; from it draw a solid arrow to a tall white outlined rectangle labeled 'Class Scores' with 'H/4 × W/4 × Ncls' above it. In the 'Encoder Block Detail' inset, draw an outer rectangle labeled 'Encoder Block' near its top-left; inside it place three adjacent inner rectangles left-to-right labeled 'Self-Attention', 'MLP Layer', 'Patch Merge' in distinct pastel fills; put '×N' near the bottom-right inside the inset. prompt: Create a clean vector multi-panel scientific figure on a white background with crisp lines and a consistent sans-serif font; the full figure is a 3-panel layout arranged in one row: (A) on the left, (B) in the center, (C) on the right, all panels equal height and aligned along their top edges; place panel labels “(A)”, “(B)”, “(C)” at the top-left corner inside each panel. Panel (A): reproduce the full hybrid neuro-symbolic reasoning architecture diagram as follows: at the top of panel (A), place three rounded light-gray boxes aligned horizontally labeled “Physics problem”, “Model popularity problem”, and “Car show puzzle” with the exact text content: Physics problem box contains “Metals conduct electricity. Insulators do not conduct electricity. If something is made of iron, then it is metal. Nails are made of iron. Is the following statement true, false, or unknown? Nails cannot conduct electricity.”; Model popularity problem box contains “No giant language model has bad performance. If a language model has good performance, it is used by some researchers. A work used by some researchers should be popular. If ModelA is a giant language model, then the same for ModelB. ModelA is a giant language model. Is the following statement true, false, or unknown? ModelB is popular.”; Car show puzzle box contains “In an antique car show, there are three vehicles: a tractor, a convertible, and a minivan. The tractor is the second-oldest. The minivan is newer than the convertible. Which of the following is true? A) The convertible is the oldest. B) The tractor is the oldest. C) The minivan is the oldest.”; centered below these three boxes place a large pale-yellow rounded rectangle labeled “Problem Formulator”; draw three solid downward arrows from each top box into “Problem Formulator”, colored green (from Physics problem), blue (from Model popularity problem), and orange (from Car show puzzle). Directly below “Problem Formulator”, place three medium rounded rectangles arranged left-to-right and aligned horizontally: left is light green titled “Logical Form 1” containing bold headings “Rules:”, “Facts:”, “Query:” and the exact lines: under Rules list “Metal(x) → ConductsElectricity(x).”, “MadeOfIron(x) → Metal(x).”, “Insulator(x) → ¬ConductsElectricity(x).”; under Facts list “MadeOfIron(Nail).” and “Insulator(Plastic).”; under Query write “ConductsElectricity(Nail)?”; middle is light blue titled “Logical Form 2” containing heading “Facts:” then the exact formulas “¬∃x(LanguageModel(x) ∧ Giant(x) ∧ ¬GoodPerformance(x)).”, “∀x(LanguageModel(x) ∧ GoodPerformance(x) → UsedByResearchers(x)).”, “∀x(UsedByResearchers(x) → Popular(x)).”, “LanguageModel(ModelA) ∧ Giant(ModelA).”, “LanguageModel(ModelB).”, “LanguageModel(ModelA) ∧ Giant(ModelA) → Giant(ModelB).”, and below them “Query:” then “Popular(ModelB)?”; right is light orange titled “Constraint Model” containing exactly: “Domain: 1 = oldest, 3 = newest.”, “Variables: tractor ∈ {1,2,3}, convertible ∈ {1,2,3}, minivan ∈ {1,2,3}.”, “Constraints: tractor == 2; minivan > convertible; AllDifferent(tractor, convertible, minivan).”; connect “Problem Formulator” to each of these three boxes with solid downward arrows matching the same green/blue/orange colors. Beneath the three logical representation boxes, add a wide dashed-border rounded rectangle spanning most of panel (A) width with label at its top-left “Symbolic Reasoner”; inside it place four small rounded rectangles arranged left-to-right: yellow “Logic Programming”, light blue “First-order Prover”, light orange “Constraint Optimization”, pink “SMT Solver”; connect “Logical Form 1” to “Logic Programming” with a solid green downward arrow, connect “Logical Form 2” to “First-order Prover” with a solid blue downward arrow, connect “Constraint Model” to “Constraint Optimization” with a solid orange downward arrow; also add a right-pointing dashed arrow from “First-order Prover” to “SMT Solver”. Directly below the Symbolic Reasoner, centered, draw a dashed-border rounded rectangle labeled “Result Interpreter”; from each of the four internal modules draw a solid downward arrow into “Result Interpreter”; inside “Result Interpreter” place three text snippets aligned left-to-right: under the arrow from “Logic Programming” write “ConductsElectricity(Nail) = True.”, under the arrow from “First-order Prover” write “Entails Popular(ModelB).”, under the arrow from “Constraint Optimization” write “{convertible:1, tractor:2, minivan:3}”. At the bottom-left of panel (A), below “Result Interpreter”, add a rounded light-gray box titled “Answer” with a small subtle checkmark icon; inside write exactly: “The statement 'Nails cannot conduct electricity' is false.”, “The statement 'ModelB is popular' is true.”, “A) The convertible is the oldest.”. Panel (B): compact ablation diagram aligned vertically: at the top of panel (B) place one rounded light-gray box titled “Input problem” containing the single short sentence “Nails are made of iron.”; below it place a pale-yellow rounded rectangle labeled “Problem Formulator”; below that place one light green rounded rectangle titled “Logical Form 1” containing only “Query: ConductsElectricity(Nail)?”; below that place a dashed-border rounded rectangle labeled “Symbolic Reasoner (single)” containing one small yellow box “Logic Programming”; below that place a dashed-border rounded rectangle labeled “Result Interpreter” containing the single line “ConductsElectricity(Nail) = True.”; connect each level with centered solid downward arrows (all green), and ensure all boxes are center-aligned in a single vertical stack. Panel (C): compact branch comparison arranged as two side-by-side mini-pipelines inside the panel: place a top-centered title text “Reasoner choice” near the top; below it create two columns aligned vertically and aligned to each other: left column header “FOL path” above a light blue rounded rectangle “Logical Form 2” (contain only “Query: Popular(ModelB)?”), then below it a dashed-border rounded rectangle “First-order Prover”, then below it a dashed-border rounded rectangle “Result Interpreter” with “Entails Popular(ModelB).”; right column header “SMT path” above a light blue rounded rectangle “Logical Form 2” (contain only “Query: Popular(ModelB)?”), then below it a dashed-border rounded rectangle “SMT Solver”, then below it a dashed-border rounded rectangle “Result Interpreter” with “Entails Popular(ModelB).”; in each column connect boxes with solid downward blue arrows; between the two middle reasoner boxes (“First-order Prover” and “SMT Solver”) add a dashed right-pointing arrow from “First-order Prover” to “SMT Solver” to indicate optional handoff; keep all text short and legible and maintain consistent styling across panels.

prompt: Create a clean academic 3-panel scientific figure on a white background with thin black vector lines, solid and dashed arrows, and black sans-serif text. Arrange panels in a two-row layout: panel (A) is a wide top panel spanning the full width; panels (B) and (C) are a bottom row with (B) on the left and (C) on the right, aligned along their top edges. Place the panel labels “(A)”, “(B)”, “(C)” in the top-left corner inside each panel. (A) Title centered at the top inside panel (A): “Instance Segmentation Pipeline” in bold. In panel (A), draw a left-to-right architecture diagram: at far left, a rectangular natural image of a cow in a field with sky at top, a tree on the horizon, and grass at bottom; overlay three horizontal translucent bands spanning the image width labeled “sky” (top), “cow” (middle), “grass” (bottom); add a short caption directly below the image: “Input image (3 x H x W)”. Immediately right of the input, add a narrow vertical area titled beside it “Box embeddings (d x N)” and show three small colored squares stacked vertically; connect input to this area with a solid rightward arrow. To the right, draw a tall rounded rectangle labeled vertically “Multi-head attention”; draw three separate solid arrows from the three squares into the left side of this block. Slightly above and to the right of the attention block, place a square blue heatmap thumbnail with a short label above: “Encoded image (d x H/32 x W/32)”; connect attention to it with a short solid arrow. Directly below the encoded image, place a vertical column of three small blue-yellow heatmap thumbnails; to the right of this column, add the caption “Attention maps (N x M x H/32 x W/32)”; connect the encoded image down to the attention-map column with a solid arrow. To the right of the encoded image/attention maps area, create a boxed section titled at its top “Backbone features”; inside, stack four heatmap rectangles vertically labeled from top to bottom “Stage 5”, “Stage 4”, “Stage 3”, “Stage 2”; draw a main solid arrow from the encoded image into the left side of this backbone stack. To the right, add a wide boxed region labeled along its top edge “FPN-style CNN”; inside, place four narrow vertical blocks in a horizontal row, each reading “Conv 3x3 + GN + ReLU”; connect backbone to the leftmost FPN block with a solid arrow, and connect each FPN block to the next with solid arrows. From each backbone stage thumbnail, draw a dashed diagonal arrow to the top of its corresponding FPN block (skip connections). To the right, draw a vertical column of four mask thumbnails (dark blue with a bright yellow blob) with a caption to the right: “Mask logits (N x H/4 x W/4)”; connect the FPN blocks to the mask column with four solid arrows. Immediately to the right, draw a tall rectangle labeled “Pixel-wise argmax” with multiple solid arrows converging from the mask thumbnails into its left edge. At the far right, show an output segmentation image of the same scene with flat regions: sky tan, tree dark green, grass light green, cow gray; overlay short labels in black: “sky”, “tree”, “cow 93%”, “grass”; add a caption centered below: “Output segmentation”. (B) In panel (B), show a compact ablation diagram titled at the top center: “Ablation: no skip connections”; depict three left-to-right boxes aligned on a single horizontal baseline: “Backbone features” (a mini vertical stack of two heatmaps labeled “Stage 5” above “Stage 2”), then “FPN-style CNN” (two small blocks in a row each labeled “Conv”), then “Mask logits” (two stacked mask thumbnails); connect these with solid rightward arrows, and explicitly omit any dashed skip arrows (none shown). (C) In panel (C), show a compact qualitative comparison titled at the top center: “Attention maps”; draw two side-by-side columns aligned vertically: left column header “Head 1” above a vertical stack of three small heatmap squares; right column header “Head 2” above a vertical stack of three small heatmap squares; place a small box labeled “Encoded image” centered above both columns with a single solid arrow going down that splits into two solid arrows, one into the top heatmap of each column; keep all labels short and readable, with consistent line weights and alignment across panels.

prompt: Create a clean academic 3-panel scientific figure on a pure white background with thin light-gray divider lines separating panels; arrange the panels as a two-column layout where panel (A) is a large panel on the left spanning the full height, and panels (B) and (C) are stacked on the right as a top panel and a bottom panel, all panel labels “(A)”, “(B)”, “(C)” placed consistently at the top-left corner of their respective panels in small black sans-serif text. Panel (A) must contain the main content as a compact 2-column by 3-row grid of six equally sized mini-panels with thin light-gray internal dividers; each mini-panel has a small label in parentheses near its own top-left corner and a short title centered at its own top, using consistent black sans-serif text; the titles from top-left to bottom-right are: (a) “Molecule”, (b) “Mass-Spring System”, (c) “Planetary System”, (d) “Rigid Body System”, (e) “Sentence and Parse Tree”, (f) “Image and Scene Graph”. Mini-panel (b) left half: a curved chain of small green disks connected by alternating dark and light spring segments forming a wavy upward-arching line, with a thicker blue disk near the right end; (b) right half: one central blue node with four yellow nodes arranged around it; draw solid black arrows from each yellow into the blue and from the blue back to each yellow, and also draw solid black arrows forming a clockwise ring connecting the four yellow nodes. Mini-panel (c) left half: an orbital-motion depiction with a bright blue ball near the center and translucent yellow, green, and purple spiral arc trails curling around it, plus one small green ball at the end of a trail; (c) right half: exactly three nodes forming a triangle (blue near top, purple bottom-left, yellow bottom-right) with double-headed solid black arrows on all three edges so all pairs are connected. Mini-panel (d) left half: a light-blue rectangular frame container with three colored discs inside (yellow lower-left, blue upper-left, purple upper-right), each with a soft translucent motion-blur streak; (d) right half: five nodes arranged like a skewed pentagon (purple upper-right, light gray right-middle, yellow lower-right, blue lower-left, green upper-left) connected mainly by thin solid black arrows pointing clockwise around the pentagon, plus at least one diagonal arrow from the purple node down toward the blue node. Mini-panel (e) left half: print the sentence “The brown dog jumped.” in black, aligned toward the left; (e) right half: a downward branching parse tree with blue circular nodes (one root at top, two nodes beneath, then one more node below) connected by dashed black arrows pointing downward; at the bottom, place four green rounded-rectangle leaf nodes aligned in a row labeled left-to-right “The”, “brown”, “dog”, “jumped”, each connected upward by dashed arrows. Mini-panel (f) left half: a 3-by-2 grid of six square image tiles separated by thin white lines, each tile containing simple rendered 3D shapes on a neutral floor including at least one red cylinder, one green cube, one blue cube, and additional colored spheres/blocks; (f) right half: a scene graph with seven circular thumbnail-like nodes arranged around a ring, each containing a tiny cropped picture of a colored object scene, with thin black dashed lines connecting every pair of nodes (dense web, no arrowheads). Panel (B) is a compact “Graphs only” ablation: inside panel (B), arrange a 2-column by 3-row grid aligned like panel (A) but containing only the right-half abstract interaction graphs for (a)–(f); each cell has the same small label (a)–(f) at top-left and a short centered title “Graph”, and each graph reproduces the same node counts, colors, and connectivity patterns as in panel (A) while omitting any concrete-system drawings; add a thin light-gray footer band along the bottom of panel (B) containing a tiny legend with three short labels aligned left-to-right: “solid arrow”, “double-headed”, “dashed line”, each accompanied by a matching miniature line sample. Panel (C) is a compact “Concrete→Graph” mapping: split panel (C) into two aligned vertical columns labeled at the top “Concrete” (left column) and “Graph” (right column); in the left column stack three small concrete examples (top: molecule from (a) left half, middle: mass-spring from (b) left half, bottom: image-tiles grid from (f) left half), and in the right column stack their corresponding graphs (top: (a) graph, middle: (b) graph, bottom: (f) graph), with each concrete example horizontally aligned with its matching graph; draw three thick light-gray rightward arrows centered between columns, each arrow connecting a concrete example to its matched graph. prompt: Create a crisp academic multi-panel scientific figure on a white background with three panels labeled '(A)', '(B)', '(C)', each label placed in the top-left corner of its panel in black sans-serif. Panel (A) contains the main comparison: inside (A), make three horizontal lanes stacked vertically, separated by gray dashed horizontal divider lines; in the top-right of each lane place a short lane title in black: 'Retriever–Reader' for the top lane, 'Direct QA' for the middle lane, and 'Generator–Reader' for the bottom lane. In all three lanes, include an orange circle with a white '?' for the input and a green circle with a white 'i' for the output; under every orange circle place the black text 'Question' and under every green circle place the black text 'Answer'; keep all question/answer circles the same size across the entire figure. In (A) top lane (Retriever–Reader), place a gray cylinder labeled 'Knowledge Base' on the far left; to its right place a blue rounded rectangle labeled 'Retriever'; connect the cylinder to the retriever with a solid black rightward arrow; place the orange Question circle above the Retriever and connect it to the top of the Retriever with a solid black downward arrow; to the right of Retriever place a white document/folder icon with a thin blue outline labeled 'Evidence' and connect Retriever to it with a solid black rightward arrow; to the right of the Evidence icon place a light orange rounded rectangle labeled 'Reader' and connect Evidence to Reader with a solid black rightward arrow; place the green Answer circle below the Reader and connect Reader to Answer with a solid black downward arrow. In (A) middle lane (Direct QA), arrange a simple left-to-right chain centered vertically in the lane: orange Question circle on the left, a yellow rounded rectangle labeled 'Direct QA Model' in the middle, and a green Answer circle on the right; connect with solid black rightward arrows from Question to model and model to Answer. In (A) bottom lane (Generator–Reader), place the orange Question circle near the top-left of the lane; directly below it place a purple rounded rectangle labeled 'Generator' and connect with a solid black downward arrow; above that arrow place the small black text 'prompt'; to the right of Generator place a white document icon with a thin black outline labeled 'Generated doc' and connect Generator to it with a solid black rightward arrow; to the right of the Generated doc icon place a light orange rounded rectangle labeled 'Reader' and connect with a solid black rightward arrow; place the green Answer circle below the Reader and connect Reader to Answer with a solid black downward arrow. Panel (B) is a compact ablation view: left-to-right, place an orange Question circle on the left, then a blue rounded rectangle labeled 'Retriever', then a light orange rounded rectangle labeled 'Reader', then a green Answer circle on the right; connect them with solid black rightward arrows; above the Retriever, place a small gray cylinder labeled 'Knowledge Base' and overlay a bold black 'X' on the cylinder; do not draw any arrow from the crossed-out Knowledge Base to the Retriever (showing KB removed), but keep the rest of the chain connected. Panel (C) is a compact parallel-branch comparison that merges: on the left side place one orange Question circle; from it draw two solid black arrows splitting into two branches, one arrow going to an upper blue rounded rectangle labeled 'Retriever' and the other arrow going to a lower purple rounded rectangle labeled 'Generator' (the two boxes stacked vertically and left-aligned); to the right of Retriever place a white document icon labeled 'Evidence' and connect with a solid black rightward arrow; to the right of Generator place a white document icon labeled 'Generated doc' and connect with a solid black rightward arrow; to the right of these two document icons place a single light orange rounded rectangle labeled 'Reader' positioned so both document icons connect into it with two separate solid black arrows that clearly converge on the Reader; place a green Answer circle below the Reader and connect Reader to Answer with a solid black downward arrow. Use readable black sans-serif text throughout, solid black arrows with clear arrowheads, consistent icon/box styling across panels, and keep all elements neatly aligned within their panels.

prompt: Create a clean academic multi-panel architecture diagram on a white background explaining three ways of enforcing logical relations between events for a language model, with strong emphasis on crisp, readable in-figure text and unambiguous labels. Beneath the blue rectangle, arrange three tall rounded-rectangle panels of equal width side by side with thin dark-gray outlines: pale green on the left, pale orange in the middle, pale yellow on the right; place panel labels near the top-left corner of each panel as 'Panel (a)', 'Panel (b)', and 'Panel (c)'. Beneath that box add the caption 'Output answers' and a small green circle with a white check mark. Add a short callout annotation pointing to the dashed constraints box: 'Callout: constraints list'. At the bottom outside the green panel, centered, place '(a) Generative-based'. In the middle pale orange panel, add the title 'Logical Rule Set'. Below it, create a light rectangle labeled 'Rule examples' with two numbered lines exactly: '(1) If two events are COREFERENCE, they cannot have TEMPORAL, CAUSAL, or SUBEVENT relations.' and '(2) If event A CAUSES event B, then A must be BEFORE or OVERLAP B.'. In the right pale yellow panel, add the title 'LLM-LR Dataset'. Beneath it, create a light rectangle labeled 'Example training instance' containing three short lines exactly: 'Context: Event A and event B are COREFERENCE, and event B and event C are COREFERENCE.', 'Question: Based on the rules, what are the four relations between event A and event C?', and 'Answers: COREFERENCE=FALSE, TEMPORAL=OVERLAP, CAUSAL=CAUSE, SUBEVENT=NONE.'. Directly below it add a small green circle with a white check mark. At the bottom outside the yellow panel, centered, place '(c) Pretraining-based'. Add a small footer line at the bottom of the overall figure as a one-sentence footnote: 'Footnote: Solid arrows show processing steps within a method, while dashed arrows denote shared input routing.'. prompt: Create a single-panel scientific workflow diagram on a white background, optimized for crisp OCR-readable in-figure text, explaining a causal reasoning pipeline with a causal graph and ATE calculations. At the very top, draw a dark blue horizontal rounded rectangle spanning most of the width with centered white sans-serif text 'Question', and from its bottom center draw a solid black downward arrow into the main body. Below the arrow, left-aligned, place a bold black title 'Causal Reasoning Chain Model' and directly beneath it a smaller multi-clause subtitle 'Subtitle: prepare inputs, solve estimand, report ATE'. On the left side, draw a tall vertical blue rounded rectangle labeled in white 'Preparation Phase'; immediately to its right stack four pastel rounded rectangles vertically with a small bold upper-left label in each: 'Step 1', 'Step 2', 'Step 3', 'Step 4'. Inside each step box, include the following short exact description lines (black text): for Step 1 include 'Extract the causal graph.'; for Step 2 include 'Determine the query type.'; for Step 3 include 'Formalize the query.'; for Step 4 include 'Gather all relevant data.' Under these four step boxes, draw a black curly brace spanning their width with a centered caption 'Inputs to causal estimation'. Below the Preparation area, draw another tall vertical blue rounded rectangle labeled in white 'Solution Phase'; to its right stack two rounded rectangles: a light purple box with label 'Step 5' and text about deducing the estimand using causal inference and do-calculus, and a pale orange box with label 'Step 6' and text about calculating the estimand using data and arithmetic; connect Step 5 to Step 6 with a short solid black downward arrow. In the upper middle-right, place a small causal graph with three white circles with thin black borders labeled 'X', 'Y', and 'Z' (black text), arranged triangularly, with solid black arrows Z→X, Z→Y, and X→Y. Add at least two short callout annotations with leader lines: one pointing to node Z reading 'Callout: confounder Z' and one pointing to the arrow X→Y reading 'Callout: causal effect X→Y'. Immediately to the right of the graph, add a formula block with a bold heading 'Average Treatment Effect' and also include the short label 'ATE'; beneath it, show the ATE equation in math style using the exact component strings 'E[Y|do(X=1)]' and 'E[Y|do(X=0)]' (compose the full equation as ATE equals the difference of those expectations). Below, list the probability lines as separate readable entries containing these exact strings: 'P(Z=1)=0.50', 'P(Z=0)=0.50', 'P(Y=1|X=1,Z=0)=0.08', 'P(Y=1|X=0,Z=0)=0.04', 'P(Y=1|X=1,Z=1)=0.09', and 'P(Y=1|X=0,Z=1)=0.06'. From the bottom of the Step 6 box, draw a solid black downward arrow to a centered math expression that clearly shows the ATE computation using the exact tokens 'ATE(X) =', 'Σ_z', 'P(Z=z)', and bracketed differences for the two conditional Y probabilities; directly beneath, place a numeric result line containing the exact strings 'ATE(X) = -0.021' and '< 0'. At the very bottom, draw a dark blue horizontal rounded rectangle spanning most of the width; on its left in white text write 'Final Q', and in the bottom instruction area include the short directive 'Answer with Yes or No'. Centered just below this bottom bar, add bold dark blue text 'Final answer: No'. Add a small footnote line at the bottom edge reading 'Footnote: probabilities sum to 1.' Ensure all arrows are solid black, typography is consistent sans-serif, all required quoted strings appear exactly as written, and no logos or external branding appear.

prompt: Create a single-panel horizontal pipeline diagram on a white background in a clean academic vector style with crisp line work and sans-serif fonts; place a top-center figure title 'Two-stage LM pipeline' with a multi-clause subtitle directly beneath it reading 'Subtitle: small LM filters candidates, large LM reranks, outputs MCQ answer'. Add a small panel tag in the upper-left corner 'Panel A'. At the top-left, draw an orange rounded rectangle with the header 'Easy example:' and inside it the sentence split across readable lines: 'The last witness,' 'Person A' 'from' 'Country X,' 'arrived early Monday.' where 'Person A' is colored blue and 'Country X' is colored yellow; centered immediately below this orange box place an italic orange label 'Easy sample'. At the top-center, draw a light pink rounded rectangle with the header 'Hard example:' and inside it the sentence split across readable lines: 'Alex met leader' 'Person B' 'from' 'City Y' 'in State Z on May 10.' where 'Person B' is blue and 'City Y' is yellow; centered immediately below it place an italic magenta label 'Hard sample'. In the middle beneath the two example boxes, draw a large white rounded rectangle with a black border titled 'Filter' and a smaller gray subtitle 'Small LM'; inside the Filter box place a simple cartoon robot head icon and label the icon with the short text 'robot'. From the Easy example box draw a solid orange downward arrow into Filter labeled 'easy input', and from the Hard example box draw a solid magenta downward arrow into Filter labeled 'hard input'. Directly below Filter, draw two small histogram plots side by side, each as a black-outlined rectangle with axis labels 'count' on the vertical side and 'bins' under the bars, and each containing exactly six solid black vertical bars; in the left histogram make the leftmost bar the tallest, and in the right histogram make the third bar from the left clearly the tallest and draw a magenta oval encircling that third bar. Under the left histogram add the caption 'task: origin' in orange; under the right histogram add the caption 'task: city_of_residence' in magenta. From the bottom of the Filter box, draw two short solid black downward arrows, one to each histogram. Add two short callout annotations with leader lines: one pointing to the robot icon reading 'Callout: Filter uses small LM', and one pointing to the magenta oval reading 'Callout: oval marks top bin'. From the right histogram draw a diagonal magenta arrow to the right toward a second major box. To the right of the Filter at the same vertical level, draw another large white rounded rectangle titled 'Reranker' with smaller gray subtitle 'Large LM'. Above the Reranker at the top-right, place two side-by-side rounded rectangles: the left is a light pink box with header 'Question' containing the short passage 'Alex met leader Person B from City Y in State Z.' followed by four lines '(a) Person B lives in State Z' '(b) Person B lives in City Y' '(c) Person B was born in City Y' '(d) Person B has no relation to City Y'; the right is a light green box with header 'Demonstration' containing the passage 'The lawyer denied reports that she cried in court.' followed by '(a) she is the client' '(b) she is the lawyer' '(c) she is unrelated to the lawyer' and then a line starting with 'Analysis:' and ending with 'Answer: (c)'. Draw a solid magenta downward arrow from Question to Reranker labeled 'question to reranker' and a solid green downward arrow from Demonstration to Reranker labeled 'demo to reranker'. Under the Reranker, add a gray rounded rectangle containing the explanation text 'The sentence implies that Person B is associated with City Y in State Z but does not mention birthplace, so Person B lives in City Y.' and beneath it a bold line 'Answer: (b)'. Add a small legend box near the bottom-right titled 'Legend' listing 'orange = easy' 'magenta = hard' 'green = demo' 'pink = question'. Add a one-sentence footnote at the very bottom reading 'Footnote: Histograms are schematic score counts for illustration.' Ensure all quoted strings appear exactly as written and are clearly legible.
